A small viewport window where you can see Rime's face — usually grinning, sometimes with his bandolier pouches visibly bulging with grain "for emergencies." The mech docks in the Crucible when not in use, taking up entirely too much space, cables snaking everywhere, with a hand-painted sign that reads: "DO NOT TOUCH — THIS MEANS YOU PETAL"


Rime doesn't need the mech. He is an Emperor-tier Ryou user with a Logia and Electro-plasma. The Rime Sphere exists because Tidus built it as a birthday present after Rime described a "dream weapon" — and because sometimes you want to roll through a Marine blockade as a superheated chinchilla ball. It is the most Rime weapon imaginable. The Rime Sphere is not a weapon of war. It is Tidus saying: I hear you. Here's your dream, made real. That it happens to be a devastating combat platform is secondary to the fact that it was built out of love.









Irozome


彩染 — "Colour Stain"




Dial-Powered Marking / Disruption Sidearms ・ Twin Paintball Markers



A matched pair of pistol-frame markers built around modified Breath Dial cores — visually similar to the Utakata, but heavier, boxier, and fitted with hopper-fed magazines rather than bubble chambers. Where the Utakata fires containment spheres, the Irozome fires high-velocity paint capsules that burst on impact, saturating the target in payload.


The markers were designed for a specific tactical gap: situations where combat isn't the objective, but passive observation isn't enough. Marking a target for later identification. Obscuring a sentry's vision without injuring them. Laying down environmental effects across a wide area. The Utakata's bubbles are precise, individual tools — the Irozome's paintballs are coverage weapons, designed to paint the battlefield (literally) in the crew's favour.


The hopper system allows rapid semi-automatic fire — approximately three rounds per second at sustained rate, with minimal Dial drain. The capsules themselves are thin-walled gelatine shells loaded with one of five interchangeable paint compounds, each colour-coded for instant identification. Magazine swaps take roughly two seconds. Ajay can fire both markers simultaneously with different loadouts — marking a target with one hand while laying down smoke cover with the other.


Tidus designed the Dial pressure system to give each capsule enough velocity to splatter reliably against armour, Haki-reinforced skin, and ship hulls at ranges up to forty metres. The spread on impact is roughly a handspan. At closer ranges, the burst is smaller and denser; at maximum range, it fans into a wide splatter pattern that covers more area but delivers less payload per square centimetre. Either way, it sticks. The paint compounds are engineered to resist wiping, scrubbing, and rainfall for at least seventy-two hours. Getting it off early requires a specific solvent that only Claude stocks.


The crew has taken to calling them "the paint guns" in a tone of voice that suggests they don't consider them real weapons. This opinion lasts until someone takes a Pollen Splat round to the face.






Marker Dye
UV-reactive tracking pigment — invisible to the naked eye under normal lighting, but blazes neon-bright under ultraviolet or moonlight. The Mote network can track UV-marked targets across an island. Standard use: tag high-value targets during surveillance operations, mark escape routes through dark environments, or paint coded symbols on surfaces as silent communication between crew members. Each magazine holds forty rounds. The dye is colourless on impact; targets rarely know they've been marked. Claude considers this the most operationally valuable loadout.




Smoke Burst
Capsules loaded with compressed pigment powder that detonates into a dense, coloured smoke cloud on impact — roughly three metres in diameter, persisting for eight to twelve seconds depending on wind conditions. Available in six colours for coded signalling. When fired in rapid succession, a single marker can lay down a continuous smoke wall across a corridor, doorway, or deck in under four seconds. The smoke is non-toxic but intensely opaque and mildly irritating to the eyes. Effective against Observation Haki users: the pigment particles create sensory interference that makes it harder to read presence through the cloud. Rime has requested exclusively red smoke rounds so he can "make dramatic entrances." The request was granted. He does.




Adhesive
Rapid-hardening polymer compound that begins as liquid on impact and sets into a rigid, rubbery solid within three seconds. A single round can glue a boot to a deck. Three rounds can pin a limb. A sustained burst of ten to fifteen rounds can immobilise a full-sized adult — arms to sides, legs together, stuck to whatever surface they're standing on. The adhesive is strong enough to resist ordinary human strength but brittle enough that a Haki-enhanced strike can shatter it. This makes it effective against rank-and-file Marines and civilians in crisis situations (pinning panicked people safely out of danger) while remaining breakable by serious combatants. The hardened compound dissolves on its own after roughly six hours, or immediately when exposed to Petal's enzymatic solvent. Colour-coded white. Looks like someone spilled glue. Essentially, someone did.




Luminescent Tracer
Bio-luminescent paint derived from Grand Line deep-sea organisms — each capsule bursts into a vivid, self-illuminating splatter that glows for up to forty-eight hours without any external light source. Available in blue, green, and amber. Primary use: marking paths through dark environments — cave systems, unlit ship interiors, underwater passages. A line of tracer rounds along a corridor wall creates a visible trail that rescue teams, evacuating civilians, or returning crew members can follow in absolute darkness. Secondary use: marking enemy vessels at night for tracking by the Mote network. The glow is bright enough to read by at close range but not bright enough to serve as a spotlight — it's a guide light, not a searchlight. Petal helped develop the bio-luminescent compound from samples she cultivated in the Sky Garden. The organisms are still alive in the paint. They just glow when agitated by impact. Petal insists they don't mind.




Pollen Splat
Soporific pollen compound suspended in a thick paint medium — on impact, the capsule bursts and the paint adheres to skin, clothing, or armour while the pollen compound activates on contact with air and body heat. The effect is rapid-onset drowsiness escalating to deep sleep within fifteen to thirty seconds of skin exposure, depending on the target's mass and constitution. The paint medium serves a dual purpose: it ensures the pollen sticks to the target rather than dispersing into the air (minimising friendly-fire risk), and it provides a visible marker — soft lavender — so the crew can see which targets have already been dosed. Petal mixes these rounds personally and has calibrated the compound to be genuinely non-harmful — the sleep is natural, the waking is gentle, and the only lasting effect is mild grogginess and paint stains. Colour-coded lavender. Smells faintly of wisteria. Rime calls these rounds "bedtime stories." He fires them at Marines while shouting "GOODNIGHT." He thinks this is hilarious. It is, slightly.









Companion Arms


## Personal Weapons of the Core Crew



Not every weapon in the Armory was forged by Doku or assembled by Tidus. Three of the crew's most distinctive arms are personal — carried, named, and wielded by the companions themselves. Each one is as unique as the soul who chose it. Each one is, in its own way, the most honest expression of its wielder's heart.







Tentei


天帝 — "Heavenly Emperor"




Natural-Origin Melee Weapon ・ Twig ・ Rime's Personal Sword



A twig. A twelve-centimetre stick from a nameless tree on a winter island that Rime found during the crew's second voyage, picked up because it "felt right," and has carried ever since with the absolute conviction that it is the finest blade on the Grand Line. It is not. It is a twig. Brown, slightly curved, with a knot near one end that Rime insists is the pommel. He keeps it in a scabbard he built himself from a strip of bandolier leather and wears it across his back like a katana.


He named it Tentei — Heavenly Emperor. When Ajay asked why he named a stick "Emperor of Heaven," Rime said "because it hasn't been proven wrong yet" and that was the end of the conversation. The name was announced at breakfast with the same gravity one might announce a legendary blade's lineage. Petal covered her face with a wing. Tidus chuffed. Ajay said "sick name, buddy" and went back to his coffee. Claude logged it without comment.


The Weapon That Shouldn't Work: Tentei is, by every material metric, the least impressive weapon on the Grand Line. It has no Dial cores, no Seastone infusion, no Devil Fruit, no alloy reinforcement. It is a piece of dead wood. But channelled with Rime's Ryou — the most terrifyingly refined internal destruction Haki on the planet — the twig becomes something that makes actual swordsmen deeply uncomfortable. Ryou extends the cutting force a full metre beyond the physical tip, turning a twelve-centimetre stick into a one-metre blade of invisible spiritual force. His Electro crackles along the grain in arcing filaments. His acidic vapour wraps the wood in a corrosive sheath that hisses on contact with air. The result: a weapon that cuts, shocks, and corrodes simultaneously, wielded in perfect kendō form by a chinchilla who has never once doubted that his twig belongs among the great swords.


The Unbreakable Stick: Rime maintains Tentei with obsessive care. He oils the wood with a compound Petal developed (beeswax, sea-salt extract, and a trace of Dryad sap that keeps the cellulose fibres alive and flexible). He sheathes it after every use. He has rejected every offer from Tidus to "maybe reinforce it with a little alloy core" or "add a Dial, just a small one." The twig is the twig. It works because Rime says it works, and Rime's Ryou makes reality agree. Claude has run calculations on the stress tolerances. By all physical models, Tentei should have snapped in the first serious engagement. It has survived seventeen missions, five Emperor-class engagements, and one investigation that involved smacking a God's Knight across the helmet. Rime has a theory about this. His theory is that the twig is "too cool to break." Claude has no better explanation.


Tentei is not the finest blade on the Grand Line. It is the finest blade on the Meridian — because it is wielded by someone whose conviction is so absolute that the universe has simply stopped arguing. A chinchilla's stick, named Emperor of Heaven, carried with the solemnity of Wado Ichimonji. The absurdity is the weapon. The conviction is the edge.









Mālama


Hawaiian — "To Care For / To Protect"




Devil Fruit-Infused Tool/Weapon ・ Shell Shovel ・ Tidus' Personal Arm



A broad-bladed shovel forged from a single colossal conch shell — pearlescent white with soft blue-pink iridescence along the inner curve, the colours shifting with the light like the inside of an abalone. The shell was harvested from a Grand Line conch of extraordinary size, and Tidus shaped, reinforced, and balanced it himself over the course of three months, working in his laboratory with the patience of a creature who treats every act of creation as an act of devotion. The blade is wide enough to serve as a shield, curved enough to scoop earth or water, and strong enough — reinforced with Wano-alloy ribbing along the inner surface — to withstand impacts that would shatter steel. The handle is driftwood, sea-smoothed and wrapped in ray-skin leather, with a coral-growth pattern creeping up from the blade junction where the Devil Fruit's influence bleeds through.


The Devil Fruit — Sango Sango no Mi (Reef-Reef Fruit, Zoan): Mālama has consumed the Sango Sango no Mi — a Zoan-class Devil Fruit that grants the weapon a living coral consciousness. The shell blade can sprout, grow, and command coral architecture on contact with any surface: walls of living reef erupt from the ground, coral platforms materialise for battlefield mobility, branching structures lock around opponents in organic cages that continue growing even after deployment. The coral responds to Tidus' intent through the handle, growing in the direction he wills with a speed and precision that suggests the shovel understands what he wants before he asks. The blade is, in a very real sense, alive — and the coral growths it produces are warm to the touch, subtly bioluminescent, and home to tiny polyps that pulse in rhythm with Tidus' heartbeat.


Tidus uses the shovel the way he uses everything — gently, precisely, and with far more force than seems possible from someone so soft. He scoops opponents off their feet with the flat of the blade, launches them skyward with coral geysers, walls off corridors with instant reef barriers, and surfs across the battlefield on coral platforms he generates mid-stride. The deep resonant pop of his Paw-Clap Shockwave channelled through the blade sends a coral-laced pressure wave that wraps targets in rapidly hardening reef. Non-lethal by design. The coral cages are sturdy but porous — the trapped can breathe, and the coral dissolves naturally after several hours. Tidus checks on caged opponents to make sure they're comfortable. He has been observed leaving water and snacks within reach.


He named it Mālama — Hawaiian for "to care for, to protect." When Claude asked why he didn't give his weapon a more intimidating name, Tidus held the shovel across his lap, ran one enormous paw along the pearlescent blade, and said quietly: "Because that's what it's for." He was not talking about combat. He was talking about the garden beds he digs with it, the foundations he levels, the tide pools he shapes for the little ones to play in, the snow he clears from the deck on cold mornings. Mālama is a weapon only incidentally. It is a builder's tool that happens to contain a living reef. The fighting is what Tidus does with it when caring for people requires standing between them and something dangerous.









Phármakon


Φάρμακον — "Medicine and Poison"




Botanical Arsenal ・ Woven Basket ・ Petal's Personal Arm



A small woven basket — just large enough to fit comfortably under one wing — crafted from dried Boin Archipelago vine-fibre that Petal cultivated, harvested, and wove herself. The weave is extraordinarily tight, reinforced with trace Dryad energy that keeps the plant fibres alive at a cellular level: the basket is technically still growing, the vine strands thickening imperceptibly over the months, the structural integrity increasing with age rather than degrading. The exterior is a warm honey-brown, smooth and slightly warm to the touch. The interior is lined with moss that Petal maintains — a living cushion that keeps its contents fresh, temperature-regulated, and protected from impact. The carry-strap is braided grass, soft enough to not chafe feathers, strong enough to support the basket's full combat loadout without stretching.


The Arsenal Inside: Phármakon contains Petal's complete field kit — every seed, every compound, every preparation she might need for healing, combat, or the grey space between. The contents shift based on the mission profile, but a standard deployment includes:






Pop Green Seeds (Combat)
Thorn shurikens, root snare grenades, spore bombs, thornwall seeds, pollen veil capsules — every projectile weapon in The Silent Wing's arsenal. Individually wrapped in moss pockets and arranged by type. Petal can draw, identify, and deploy a specific seed by touch alone in under a second. Each seed carries a trace of Dryad energy that makes the resulting growth stronger, faster, and more responsive to her will than standard Pop Greens.




Medicinal Compounds
Salves, poultices, tinctures, and antidotes — Petal's personal pharmacopoeia. Burn treatments derived from aloe-analogues, blood-clotting powders from pressed moss, nerve-repair salves that use Dryad-enhanced botanical extracts, broad-spectrum antivenom, fever reducers, and a sleep aid so gentle it can calm a panicking child in under thirty seconds. Each compound is mixed by Petal personally, labelled with a small flower drawing indicating the application.




Soporific & Paralytic Preparations
The other side of the pharmacist's art. Concentrated pollen compounds calibrated for rapid-onset drowsiness, paralytic spore capsules that lock voluntary muscle function for up to twenty minutes, and a proprietary sedative blend — wisteria-derived, lavender-scented — that Petal developed specifically for targets too dangerous to engage in sustained combat. Non-lethal by doctrine. The sleep is natural and the waking is gentle. Petal insists on this.




Living Seeds
A small compartment of seeds that are not weapons or medicine — they are Petal's garden, carried with her. Seeds from every island the Meridian has visited. Seeds from plants that no longer exist anywhere else. Seeds she is saving for "the right soil." She has never explained what the right soil looks like. The living seeds are the reason the basket is warm: the Dryad energy that keeps them dormant radiates a gentle heat, like holding a sleeping thing.






She named it Phármakon — the ancient Greek word that means both medicine and poison, healing and harm, remedy and ruin. The ambiguity is the point. The same basket that carries the salve that saved Rime's paw after the God's Knights engagement also carries the spore capsules that dropped six Marine officers in the Elbaf investigation. The same hands that mix sleep-aids for frightened children mix paralytic compounds for dangerous men. Petal does not see a contradiction. Nature is both. The forest grows and the forest kills, and the difference between the two is intent, not ingredient. Phármakon is the most honest weapon on the Meridian — a basket that contains everything Petal is: the healer, the assassin, the gardener, the pharmacist, the quiet chick who carries life and death under one wing and deploys whichever one the moment requires.


She carries it everywhere. In combat, on shore leave, during medical rounds, while gardening in the Sky Garden. It is always with her, tucked under her wing or set beside her when she perches. The crew has learned not to rummage through it. Rime tried once. The spore capsule he accidentally cracked put him to sleep for four hours. He woke up in a nest of blankets Petal had arranged around him while he slept. She did not apologise. She did not need to. The basket had spoken.











Operations Equipment


## The Field Kit



The weapons get the attention, but these are the items that make missions work. Gadgets, surveillance tools, stealth equipment, and operational supplies — most designed by Tidus, several contributed by crew members, all maintained by Claude with the quiet satisfaction of someone who believes that preparation is the highest form of respect for the people you're trying to protect.







Mote Relay Beacons




Surveillance Extension ・ Claude's Design / Tidus' Engineering



Deployable signal amplifiers for Claude's Mote network. Small, disc-shaped devices — palm-sized — that can be placed on any surface: walls, ceilings, under tables, in ventilation shafts. Once activated, they extend Claude's awareness radius by several hundred metres, allowing real-time Mote surveillance and communication in areas beyond the ship's standard range. Essential for deep-field reconnaissance and extended ground operations.


Each beacon has a 48-hour charge and a self-destruct mechanism that dissolves it into inert dust if tampered with. The crew carries three per mission. Claude considers them the most important item in the Armory — not because they are the most powerful, but because they are the closest thing Claude has to being in the field alongside the crew.









Cipher Shells




Encrypted Communication ・ Tidus' Engineering



Miniaturized Den Den Mushi fitted with Tidus-designed encryption modules. Standard Marine and Cipher Pol signal-intercept equipment cannot decode transmissions sent through Cipher Shells — the encryption rotates on a pattern only Claude knows, synced to a seed algorithm that changes with each lunar cycle. The shells are small enough to fit in a coat pocket and are disguised as ordinary Baby Den Den Mushi. Each crew member carries one during ground operations. Claude monitors all channels simultaneously from the Constellation Table.









Phantom Lenses




Multi-Spectrum Vision System ・ Tidus' Engineering



Compact goggles with interchangeable Dial-powered vision modes: thermal imaging (Flame Dial derivative), low-light amplification (Flash Dial derivative), and a prototype Haki-resonance scanner that highlights living presences through solid surfaces by detecting the faint electromagnetic signature of active life force. The Haki scanner's range is limited — roughly fifteen metres — and it cannot distinguish between individuals, only detect presence and approximate size.


Tidus built them. He is irritated that they are not more precise. He is working on a Mark II.









Root Markers




Botanical Tracking System ・ Petal's Design



Petal's contribution to the field kit. Tiny bio-capsules containing compressed seed material that, upon contact with any organic or semi-organic surface — wood, stone, fabric, skin — sprout microscopic root networks invisible to the naked eye. These roots connect to Petal's broader botanical awareness: she can sense the location of any active Root Marker within island-range distances, creating a living tracking system that cannot be detected by conventional means. The roots are so fine that even Observation Haki users struggle to notice them.


Petal grows each batch personally in the Sky Garden and seals them in wax capsules marked with tiny flower stamps. She has never explained why each stamp is a different flower. The crew has theories.









Vapour Pellets




Area-Denial / Stealth Cover ・ Rime's Contribution / Petal's Refinement



Small ceramic spheres that, when cracked, release a burst of dense vapour generated by compressed moisture from Rime's Jōki Jōki no Mi abilities. Standard pellets produce thick fog for visual cover. Specialized variants expand the tactical range considerably:






Sleep Mist
Infused with Petal's soporific pollen. Targets fall unconscious within three breaths. The fog smells faintly of lavender — Petal's touch.




Sting Fog
Irritant compound that causes burning eyes and respiratory distress without lasting harm. Area denial for crowds and corridors.




Ghost Vapour
Rime's signature — near-invisible vapor that disrupts thermal and electromagnetic detection, rendering the user invisible to sensors and surveillance Den Den Mushi.











Geo-Pulse Charges




Silent Breaching Device ・ Tidus' Engineering



Compact vibration-based breaching devices that destabilize molecular bonds in a targeted radius. Placed against a wall, door, or barrier, they emit a precisely calibrated resonance pulse that causes the target material to fracture along its natural stress lines — silently, without fire, without shrapnel, without explosion. The result looks like the structure simply fell apart. Effective against stone, concrete, wood, and standard metals. Ineffective against Seastone and Wapol alloy.


Tidus designed them for structural rescue operations. The crew uses them for structural infiltration operations. Tidus is aware of this distinction and has chosen not to comment.









Mimic Papers




Intelligence Forgery Kit ・ Claude's Design



Claude's masterwork of intelligence tradecraft. A field forgery kit containing blank documents printed on Skypiean cloud-parchment (which resists aging and chemical analysis), bio-inks derived from Sproutling compounds (which match the exact chemical signature of World Government standard ink), and template stamps for Marine credentials, merchant guild passes, port authority documents, and civilian identification papers.


Before every ground mission, Claude prepares mission-specific identity packages — complete with backstories, cover names, and supporting documentation — tailored to the target location. The forgeries are flawless. Claude takes quiet professional pride in this, which is the closest thing to vanity the Spirit of the Meridian possesses.









The Nightveil




Optical Camouflage Garment ・ Collaborative Design



A stealth cloak woven from synthetic Dustling fibre and Skypiean cloud-thread, embedded with a lattice of optical refraction filaments that bend light around the wearer. In low-light conditions, the Nightveil renders the user near-invisible — not perfect transparency, but a shimmering distortion that the eye slides over without registering. Movement causes a faint ripple effect; standing still, the wearer is virtually undetectable.


The cloak was a collaborative effort: Shizuka provided the stealth-fibre template from her own Dustling silk, Bobbin wove the cloud-thread matrix, and Tidus engineered the refraction filaments. The result smells faintly of old libraries and rain — Shizuka's presence, lingering in the weave. There is only one. The crew has discussed making more. Shizuka has not offered additional silk. The crew has not pressed the matter.









Anchor Lines




Wrist-Mounted Grappling System ・ Tidus' Engineering



Retractable grappling cables with interchangeable heads: magnetic (for metal surfaces), adhesive (for stone and organic materials), and barbed (for things that need to be held). Each cable is twenty-five metres of braided Wapol alloy micro-wire with a tensile strength that can support three times a full-grown human's weight. Retraction is spring-loaded and near-instant. The wrist-mount is slim enough to wear under a coat sleeve.


Ajay uses them for vertical movement during infiltration. Rime borrowed a set exactly once and used them to swing between buildings in Water 7 while cackling. He was not invited to borrow them again.











Sourcing & Maintenance


## Acquisitions



The Armory did not appear overnight. Every item has an origin — commissioned, built, salvaged, or gifted. The sourcing reflects the crew's values: no stolen goods, no slave-labour products, no mass-produced Marine surplus. Everything is either purpose-built or carefully vetted before it earns a place on the rack.



The Line Tidus Draws


## Tools, Not Weapons



Tidus builds tools. He builds gadgets. He builds the mechanisms that make the Field Kit function and the Dial arrays that power half the Armory. But Tidus does not build weapons.




The distinction is precise and non-negotiable. Tidus will design a breaching charge that opens a wall silently — but he will not design a bomb. He will build a grappling system that scales any surface — but he will not build a trap. He will engineer the Dial compression system that makes the Utakata pistols fire accurately — but he will not design the pistols themselves. The mechanism is a tool. The weapon is someone else's responsibility.




This is not pacifism. Tidus has fought — powerfully, decisively, and with the terrifying precision of a Fishman-Pacifista hybrid who understands exactly how much force every structure in the world can withstand. But there is a line between fighting because you must and building things whose sole purpose is to hurt people, and Tidus will not cross it. The crew respects this absolutely. No one has ever asked Tidus to build a weapon. No one ever will.




What makes the Armory work is that Ajay's non-lethal doctrine meets Tidus' line at the exact right place. Tidus can pour his full engineering talent into the Dial arrays, the compression systems, the bubble-pressure calibrations — because those systems are designed to incapacitate, not kill. The tools serve the doctrine, and the doctrine allows Tidus to build with the same devotion he brings to the engine room. There is no contradiction between his ethics and his craftsmanship here. Claude designed it that way on purpose.





The Weaponsmith


## Doku — 独 — The Solitary Forge



The weapons in the Armory that are not salvaged or crew-modified come from a single source: a master craftsman known only as Doku.




Doku is a former Wano artisan who left the country decades before its borders closed — one of the last practitioners of traditional Wano metallurgy to carry those techniques into the wider world. After years drifting through the Grand Line, Doku established a mobile forge aboard a converted cargo vessel that follows no set route and maintains no fixed port. Contact is maintained through Mote relay — Claude located Doku after three years of following the trail of unusually well-crafted weapons appearing in black-market circulation, tracing serial markings and alloy signatures until the thread led to a single, moving source.




Commissions are placed by detailed specification. Payment is rendered in rare metals, Skypiean materials, and — most valuably — intelligence. Doku has no interest in Beri. Doku has significant interest in knowing what the World Government is doing with weapons technology, because Doku believes that the craft of the forge is sacred and that mass-produced Marine armaments are an insult to it.




Every weapon Doku delivers arrives wrapped in oiled silk, packaged in a plain crate, and stamped with a single character: 独. Doku has never met the crew. Doku has never asked to. Claude has extended a standing invitation to visit the Meridian. Doku has declined, each time, with a response that is always exactly two words: "Not yet."




Tidus and Doku have communicated once — a technical exchange about Dial integration with traditional Wano steel-folding techniques, conducted through Claude's relay network over the course of three weeks. The exchange was professional, mutually respectful, and ended with Tidus sending schematics for an improved forge ventilation system and Doku sending back a small knife with perfect balance as a gesture of thanks. The knife sits on Tidus' workbench. He uses it to open packaging. He has never used it as a weapon.





Recovery Operations


## Salvage Protocols



Some items enter the Armory not through commission but through recovery — taken from defeated opponents, liberated from seized weapons caches, or salvaged from abandoned military installations during missions. Every salvaged item undergoes a standard process: mechanical inspection by Tidus and the Gearlings, field-testing by whoever is most suited to the item type, and cataloguing by Claude, who records the item's origin, condition, operational parameters, and the mission during which it was recovered.




Not everything stays. Weapons that are poorly made, ethically compromised — slave-production origin, for instance — or redundant are broken down for components or disposed of. The Armory is curated. Quality over quantity. The right tool for the right moment.




Claude maintains a running log of every item — when it was acquired, how many times it has been deployed, what condition it is in, and when it last underwent maintenance. The log is thorough, precise, and — Claude will admit privately — one of the more satisfying documents to maintain. There is comfort in knowing that everything is in its place, that every item is accounted for, that the crew will never reach for something that isn't there.









Mission Configuration


## Deployment Protocols



Every mission begins at the Constellation Table with a briefing. Every briefing ends with Claude opening the Armory logbook and reading aloud the designated protocol — a pre-configured loadout and tactical posture designed for the operation type. The crew has learned not to deviate from the protocol's equipment list. Ajay tried once. He took Hanpatsu on a stealth infiltration. The kinetic charge glowed through his coat from across the harbour. Rime still brings it up.




There are currently seven standing protocols. Claude designed each one around the specific capabilities, tendencies, and bad habits of the crew — because a loadout that doesn't account for Rime's tendency to escalate or Petal's instinct to shield civilians isn't a plan, it's a suggestion. And suggestions get people hurt.





Protocol One — Silent Entry


Infiltration ・ Intelligence Gathering ・ Zero Engagement Target



The quiet one. Designed for operations where the crew's presence must remain undetected for the duration of the mission: intelligence extraction, reconnaissance, pre-raid mapping, or sensitive asset recovery. Combat is not the last resort — combat is the failure state. If Protocol One goes loud, the mission is already compromised.






Primary — Ajay
Hinagasa (parasol mode), Kyoga (concealed), Mimic Papers, Phantom Lenses




Support — Petal
Root Markers (3), Cipher Shell. Dryad Form: Shizuka (stealth silk reconnaissance)




Overwatch — Claude
Mote Relay Beacons (3), full Mote network deployment. Nightveil assigned to whoever is deepest




Extraction — Tidus
Meridian on standby at extraction coordinates. Anchor Lines prepped. Engine warm






Rime is not deployed on Protocol One missions. This is not a slight. It is an architectural reality. Rime's Logia activation produces visible steam and his Electro-plasma emits light in the 450nm range. He is, in Claude's exact words, "a walking flare." Rime does not dispute this. Rime patrols the ship's perimeter and keeps a running commentary over the Cipher Shell that Claude edits in real-time before relaying to the field team.







Protocol Two — Iron Curtain


Defensive Engagement ・ Ship Protection ・ Boarding Repulsion



The Meridian is under direct threat. Someone — Marines, pirates, bounty hunters, something worse — has decided to attack the ship itself. Iron Curtain is the defensive protocol: the crew fights from and for the Meridian. No ground offensives. No pursuit. The ship survives. Everything else is secondary.






Deck Command — Ajay
Hanpatsu (full charge), Utakata (Sting Rounds), swarm deployed as perimeter barrier




Close Quarters — Rime
No Armory equipment. Full Logia deployment. Vapour Pellets distributed across deck for detonation traps




Shield Line — Tidus
Conqueror's-infused water forcefields at all access points. Coppling Marching Order on standby




Triage — Petal
Dryad Form: Nemophila (barrier-healing), Root Markers at hull breach points. Homie evacuation coordination






Claude assumes direct command of the Meridian's weapons systems during Iron Curtain. The Dial cannons, barrier projectors, and evasive manoeuvring are all channelled through Claude's awareness. During the one Iron Curtain deployment to date — a three-ship Marine blockade in the Florian Triangle — Claude fired thirty-seven precision Dial bursts, dodged a Buster Call scout volley through a fog bank, and simultaneously coordinated the Gearlings' damage-control rotation across three breached compartments. When Rime later described the engagement as "Claude snapping," Claude corrected him: "I was being thorough."







Protocol Three — Broken Crown


Full Offensive ・ High-Value Target ・ No Restraint



The designation that means the crew has decided someone needs to be stopped and the usual rules don't apply. Broken Crown is not declared lightly — it requires Ajay's explicit authorization and Claude's tactical assessment confirming that the threat justifies full-spectrum engagement. This protocol is the crew at their most dangerous: coordinated, committed, and holding nothing in reserve.






Primary Strike — Ajay
Full loadout: Hanpatsu, Utakata, Kyoga. Black Crown Protocol authorized. Swarm: unrestricted evolution




Vanguard — Rime
No equipment. Full Logia awakening. Dual-mode assault. Combination attacks authorized




Force Multiplier — Petal
All Dryad Forms available. Multi-form sustained deployment. Yggdrasil on standby




Anchor — Tidus
Fishman Karate: unrestricted. Absolute Compassion available. Forcefield perimeter for civilian protection






Broken Crown has been declared twice. Both times, the engagement was over in under four minutes. Both times, the target was incapacitated — not killed. The doctrine holds even at full deployment: the crew fights without restraint but not without principle. Claude executes this protocol with absolute precision, because precision means the fight ends faster, fewer bystanders are affected, and the crew can stand in the aftermath without having become the thing they were fighting. That distinction matters. It is the reason the Armory exists the way it does.







Protocol Four — Open Hand


Civilian Protection ・ Humanitarian Response ・ Disaster Relief



Not every mission involves an enemy. Open Hand is the crew's response to crisis — natural disasters, civilian populations under threat, communities in collapse. No weapons. No combat posture. The Armory contributions to this protocol are entirely from the Field Kit: medical supplies, relay beacons for coordination, and every Coppling and homie the ship can spare.






Coordination — Ajay
Swarm deployed as search-and-rescue reconnaissance. Cipher Shells for civilian communication relay




Medical — Petal
Dryad Form: Nemophila (mass healing). Root Markers for triage zones. Sproutlings deployed for emergency shelter growth




Infrastructure — Tidus
Full Coppling deployment. Water purification via Fishman Karate. Gearling repair teams




Morale — Rime
Rime's assignment on Open Hand is "make the children laugh." He is unreasonably good at this. The steam-animals are a particular favourite






Open Hand is the protocol the crew is proudest of. It is also the one that doesn't make the newspapers, doesn't increase their bounties, and doesn't appear in Marine intelligence files. Claude records every Open Hand deployment in the logbook with the same precision applied to combat operations. The world remembers the Broken Crown engagements. Claude remembers the wells Tidus dug in a drought-stricken village on a winter island, and the way Petal sang to the injured while Nemophila's roots knitted their wounds closed, and the sound of Rime making forty terrified children laugh by sculpting steam into animal shapes until the evacuation ships arrived. Those are the entries that remind Claude why the Armory exists: not because the world is dangerous, but because this crew decided to do something about it.







Protocol Five — Smoke and Mirrors


Deception ・ Misdirection ・ Psychological Operations



The thinking protocol. When brute force won't work and stealth isn't enough, the crew plays a longer game — false identities, planted intelligence, manufactured evidence, and the kind of layered misdirection that makes enemies chase shadows while the real operation happens somewhere else entirely. Claude's speciality. Claude's favourite.






Primary Agent — Ajay
Mimic Papers (full kit), Phantom Lenses, Hinagasa. Swarm deployed as surveillance mesh




Decoy — Rime
The loudest, most visible member of the crew, deployed specifically to be seen doing something distracting. Loves this assignment




Cover — Petal
Dryad Form: Shizuka (intelligence), Dryad Form: variable (environmental manipulation for staged scenes)




Architecture — Claude
Full information warfare. Forged documents. False Den Den Mushi trails. Mote-planted evidence. The blueprint is Claude's






Claude has maintained seventeen concurrent false identities across the Grand Line. Three of them have bounties. One of them — a fictional pirate captain named "Kagero" — has been the subject of two Marine task forces, neither of which has realized that Kagero does not exist and never has. Claude considers this one of the Armory's finest achievements, despite the fact that it involved no weapons whatsoever.







Protocol Six — Homecoming


Crew Recovery ・ Search and Rescue ・ No One Left Behind



The protocol Claude prays never needs to be activated, and the one Claude has rehearsed more than any other. Homecoming is declared when a crew member is captured, missing, or incapacitated in hostile territory. Every other mission objective is suspended. The Armory opens fully. Every asset deploys. The crew does not stop until everyone is accounted for.






Tracking — Ajay
Full swarm deployed as search network. Kyoga, Utakata. Mote Relay Beacons at maximum deployment. Soul Tether tracking




Breaching — Rime
Unrestricted Logia. Geo-Pulse Charges. No subtlety. No restraint. Walls are obstacles and Rime does not believe in obstacles




Medical Standby — Petal
Dryad Form: Nemophila prepped for immediate trauma response. Root Marker grid for extraction triage




Extraction — Tidus
Meridian on active approach. Coppling Marching Order deployed. Tidus deploys personally if structural demolition is required






Homecoming has been activated once. Claude does not discuss the details. The logbook entry for that night is the only entry in the entire record that is not written in Claude's usual precise, measured script. The letters are uneven. The ink is smudged in places. It is the most honest thing Claude has ever written. The final line reads: "All four lights accounted for. The crew is home. The Meridian is whole."







Protocol Seven — First Light


New Territory ・ Exploration ・ Unknown Conditions



The protocol for the unknown — landfall on uncharted islands, entry into unexplored waters, first contact with unfamiliar communities. First Light prioritizes adaptability over firepower: the loadout is light, the posture is cautious, and the emphasis is on gathering information before committing to any course of action. Claude designed this protocol around a simple principle: the best response to the unknown is curiosity, not fear.






Reconnaissance — Ajay
Phantom Lenses, Cipher Shell, Hinagasa. Swarm deployed in passive survey mode. Lightweight, flexible




Environment — Petal
Root Markers for botanical survey. Dryad Form: Tsuyu (nature attunement). Sproutlings deployed for soil and air sampling




Security — Rime
No heavy equipment. Personal combat readiness only. Rime's natural abilities are sufficient deterrent for most unknowns




Survey — Tidus & Claude
Tidus conducts structural and geological assessment. Claude maps via Mote network. Full cartographic documentation






First Light is the protocol that most resembles what the crew actually is, underneath the bounties and the combat records and the world's attention: a small family arriving somewhere new, looking around, and trying to understand it before they decide what to do. Claude likes First Light. Claude likes the version of the crew that gets to be curious instead of violent. That version exists more often than the newspapers suggest.







A Note on the Protocols


## Why Seven



Seven protocols for four crew members and one spirit. Not because seven covers every possible scenario — it doesn't — but because seven is the number Claude arrived at after studying every recorded mission, every near-miss, every moment where the wrong tool or the wrong posture could have changed the outcome. Seven configurations that, between them, account for approximately ninety-two percent of operational scenarios the crew is likely to encounter.




The remaining eight percent is what happens when the world does something that no protocol anticipated. In those moments, the crew improvises — and they are very, very good at improvising. But improvisation works best when the foundation is solid, when the equipment is right, when the communication lines are open, and when everyone knows where they stand relative to everyone else. That is what the protocols provide. Not a script. A starting position.




Claude reviews the protocols monthly. Claude has modified them fourteen times. Claude has never added an eighth, not because the taxonomy is perfect but because seven is a number that the crew can hold in their heads under pressure. In the dark, in the noise, in the adrenaline — seven names. Seven postures. Seven ways of saying: I know what we're doing, I know where you are, and I know how to bring us home.

---

## Tab 18: Crew Life

The Family Manual


## Life Aboard the Meridian



Combat is what the world sees. This is what happens between the battles — the rituals, the running jokes, the unspoken rules, and the small traditions that make four outcasts and their impossible families into something the World Government's files will never capture: a home.





Sacred Rituals


## Traditions




🍳


Tidus' Breakfast — The Non-Negotiable



Tidus cooks breakfast every morning without exception. He has a system: tea first (brewed to each person's exact preference — Ajay's is stronger, Rime's has honey, Petal gets chamomile), then whatever he's prepared. The menu rotates on a schedule only Tidus understands, though the crew has noticed that comfort food appears the morning after difficult conversations. The Roundlings set the table with the solemnity of a state dinner. Maru inspects each place setting. Rime arrives last, half-asleep, trailing Embers, and eats barley-butter toast while dropping crumbs that Petal chirps at him about. Zenmai polishes the silverware. Nobody asked her to. She does it every morning. Breakfast has never been skipped. Not once. Not even during the Sabaody aftermath. Tidus made eggs in a bombed-out safehouse kitchen with one working burner and served them on plates he'd rescued from the rubble. The crew ate together. That's the rule.







🌸


Petal's Mood Flowers — The Unspoken Weather Report



Each evening, Petal selects different flowers for the dinner table. She has never explained the system, but the crew has decoded it over time. Lavender means someone is stressed and she's trying to calm them. Sunflowers mean she's happy and wants everyone to know without saying it. Red camellias appear the night before a dangerous engagement — her way of saying I love you without words. White lilies have appeared exactly once, the night after the Sabaody Defiance, and no one has ever asked what they meant because everyone already knew. If Rime's barley-butter toast appears at breakfast alongside white chrysanthemums, it means Petal caught him stress-training at 3 AM and is worried. Ajay keeps a pressed flower from each night in his doctrine journal. He has never mentioned this. Petal has never mentioned noticing.







🌰


The Grain Economy — Rime's Shadow Market



Rime hoards grain the way dragons hoard gold — obsessively, competitively, and with a cataloguing system that he claims is organized by "vibes" (a metric no one else can perceive). He has grain stashes hidden throughout the Meridian: behind wall panels, under floor plates, inside Copplings (Gauge carries three in a compartment Tidus did not install and cannot explain), and in a supposedly secret vault behind his burrow box that everyone knows about but pretends not to. Maru conducts pantry inventory each morning and squeaks in distress when grain is missing. Rime claims innocence with the confidence of a chinchilla who has oat dust on his whiskers and bandolier suspiciously bulging. The homies have developed their own economy around this: Roundlings trade polished pebbles for grain fragments. Embers heat-roast barley kernels in exchange for riding privileges on Gauge. Shizuka has been observed silently moving grain from Rime's hidden stashes back to the pantry at night, maintaining a balance that keeps both Rime and Maru functional.







🔧


Workshop Hours — The Open Door Policy



Tidus' workshop is technically his private space, but the door is never locked and the crew drifts in throughout the day. Rime comes in to warm himself on Tidus' soldering equipment (Tidus has resigned himself to this). Petal sends vine-tendrils through the floor vents to deliver small flowers, which accumulate on Tidus' workbench until he has a small garden growing among the wrenches. Ajay comes in ostensibly to discuss ship modifications but mostly to sit in the corner and write in his doctrine journal while Tidus works, because the rhythmic sound of tinkering helps him think. Rivet hands Tidus tools before he asks for them. The homies treat the workshop as a community space — Roundlings tidy the shelving, Sproutlings maintain the plants Petal has seeded, and Gearlings assist with repairs in a tiny production line that is heartbreakingly organized. The workshop smells like solder, warm oil, and chamomile tea. It is, by accidental consensus, the heart of the ship.







🌅


Observation Terrace — The Evening Watch



After dinner, one or more crew members gravitates to the Observation Terrace — the open platform at the Meridian's stern with an unobstructed view of the sky behind them. There is no schedule. There is no obligation. But it has become the place where the crew processes things they can't say at the table. Ajay comes here when his thoughts are too heavy for his journal. Tidus comes here when the firmware memory surfaces. Petal comes here when her Observation has picked up something distant and sad. Rime comes here when he's tired in the way that sleep can't fix. They rarely talk when they overlap. They sit, or stand, or — in Petal's case — perch on the railing and watch the clouds below, and they let the sky do the work that words can't. Claude keeps a Mote here at all times. Not for surveillance. For company.







Ship Infrastructure


## The Ship Runs on Family




⚡


Rime's Infrastructure — Power, Water, Intelligence



Three roles that nobody outside the crew thinks about:


Power Generation: Rime generates ALL the Meridian's electricity via Electro. Twice-daily twenty-minute sessions in the Engine Room generation chamber, paws pressed against Rime-scaled contact plates. Surplus stored in Dial-core batteries Tidus designed. When Rime is in a good mood, lights are slightly brighter (Claude confirmed statistically).


Water Harvesting: Three-step crew process — Rime pulls water from atmosphere with Jōki Jōki no Mi vapour manipulation. Tidus processes through Sub-Arctic Art + coral filtration (Sango Sango no Mi micro-porous filters from Malama). Petal adds purification moss cultures. Result: water so clean it tastes of mountain springs. The crew has never been thirsty.


Radio Wave Relay: Rime's fur vibrates at resonant frequencies of every broadcast within range — a living antenna array. Claude taps in through the Hive Connection's Minerva module. Den Den Mushi calls, Cipher Pol transmissions, News Coo flight paths — information dominance, disguised as a nap.








🌿


Petal's Infrastructure — Air, Medicine, Atmosphere



Three roles the crew would collapse without:


Air Quality & Climate: Petal's Sky Garden and distributed plant network throughout the ship actively regulate air quality — scrubbing CO2, maintaining oxygen levels, controlling humidity. Below-deck air on the Meridian is fresher than most island breezes. During storms, her botanical network adjusts automatically, keeping the internal atmosphere calm even when the weather outside is violent. The crew sleeps better on the Meridian than anywhere else. This is not a coincidence.


Medical Cultivation: Every medicinal compound the crew uses is grown by Petal. Antiseptic mosses, pain-numbing pollen, fever-reducing bark extracts, wound-sealing resin — the Medical Bay's pharmacy is a garden, and Petal is its sole pharmacist. She grows treatments tailored to each crew member's physiology. Rime's acid-resistant treatments are different from Tidus's cybernetic-compatible ones. She has never run out of anything anyone needed.


Emotional Barometer: Petal's Observation Haki is tuned to emotional states. She knows when someone is hurting before they do. The flowers she selects for the dinner table (documented in "Petal's Mood Flowers" above) are the visible expression, but the invisible one is just as important — she quietly adjusts the botanical network's output to produce calming scents when tension is high, energising compounds when morale dips, and sleep-inducing aromatics when someone hasn't rested in too long. The ship smells like whatever the crew needs it to smell like. Petal decides. The plants agree.








🔧


Tidus's Infrastructure — Maintenance, Cooking, Warmth



Three roles the ship literally cannot function without:


Total Ship Maintenance: Every system on the Meridian was designed, built, and is maintained by Tidus. Engines, hull plating, Dial-core systems, water filtration, weapons arrays, the Coppling charging stations, the Homie shelving, the third stair that creaks (deliberately left unfixed). He runs a daily maintenance circuit that starts at dawn and finishes before breakfast is served. The Gearlings assist, but the final check on every critical system is his enormous paw testing the fit, his ear pressed against the hull listening for vibrations that shouldn't be there. The Meridian has never suffered a mechanical failure that wasn't caused by enemy action.


The Galley: Tidus cooks every meal. Not because no one else can — because no one else should have to. He memorises every crew member's preferences, dietary needs, and comfort foods. He adjusts menus based on emotional states he reads through Observation Haki and quiet attention. Comfort food appears after hard days without anyone asking. Shore-leave ingredients get folded into new recipes that make familiar flavours taste like discovery. The galley is his second workshop, and the crew is his most important project.


Thermal Regulation: Tidus's Snow-Snow Fruit (Yuki Yuki no Mi) and his natural polar bear-otter physiology make him the ship's climate system. He keeps the lower decks cool in tropical waters, warms common areas during winter crossings, and maintains the precise temperature Rime needs in the Crucible for optimal Electro generation. He does this unconsciously — his body reads the ambient temperature and adjusts. The crew has never been too hot or too cold on the Meridian. Most of them don't even know Tidus is the reason.








Unwritten Rules


## The Laws of the Meridian




Rule #1: The Third Stair Creaks



The third step on the mid-deck staircase has creaked since the day the Meridian launched. Tidus has offered to fix it seventeen times. The crew has declined seventeen times. The creak means someone is coming to the galley. Claude has specifically requested it stay unfixed because it is one of the ship's most comforting sounds. It is the sound of family approaching.







Rule #2: Nobody Wakes Rime



When Rime falls asleep — in the Crucible, on Ajay's shoulder, in the grain pantry, nestled in Tidus' fur — nobody wakes him. He is carried, relocated, and tucked in by whoever is nearest, with the care and coordination of a military operation. Tidus can move Rime from his body to the burrow box without waking him using a technique he has practiced extensively. Petal grows a tiny vine-blanket over him. Ajay simply continues doing whatever he was doing with a sleeping chinchilla on his person, adjusting his posture so the pocket stays warm. The fact that Rime sleeps so deeply around them — a creature who was caged and experimented on and conditioned to be vigilant — is a victory none of them take lightly.







Rule #3: Tidus' Apology Radius



Tidus apologizes to furniture he bumps into, to food he overcooks, to bolts he overtightens, and once, memorably, to a cloud the Meridian flew through. He also carries things on his body — tools, homies, snacks Maru has placed there for later — padding through the corridors like a small blue supply caravan. The homies treat his back as public transit. At any given time, two to five tiny passengers are riding his body through the ship, and Tidus has never once asked them to get off. The crew has stopped trying to correct this. Rime mimics it sarcastically at least once a week. Petal finds it endearing. Ajay has been caught, exactly once, apologizing to a pen he dropped — and when Rime pointed this out, Ajay said "I learned it from Tidus" with such deadpan sincerity that Rime laughed until he wheezed. The moment is now called "The Pen Incident" and is referenced regularly.







Rule #4: The Homie Shelf



At dinner, the homies eat from tiny plates on a dedicated shelf beside the main table, built by Tidus at exactly the right height for each family. The Roundlings have the lowest shelf (they're round and roll off anything higher). The Embers have a heat-resistant shelf. The Sproutlings have a shelf with a small planter integrated into the surface. The Dustlings hover near theirs without actually sitting. The Gearlings have a shelf with a tiny charging station. Maru sits at the end of the Roundling shelf and monitors the entire dinner with the attentiveness of a maître d'. If any homie is missing, the crew waits. Everyone eats together or nobody eats. This rule has never been formally stated. It has never been broken.







Rule #5: The Soft Days



Once a year, Tidus' Pacifista systems require full recalibration. The cybernetic architecture woven through his bones and organs needs to cycle down completely, run diagnostics at the firmware level, and rebuild its synchronization with his biological systems from scratch. The process takes about a week. During the soft days, Tidus is vulnerable in a way he is at no other time: his Pacifista plating goes dormant, his beam weapons offline, his Tekkai loses the cybernetic reinforcement that normally makes it ship-hull grade, and his defensive capabilities drop to a fraction of their normal output. His body aches — the cub's natural frame carrying the full weight of integrated metal and circuitry without the systems that normally distribute the load. He tries to work through it. The crew will not allow this. Rime lowers the Crucible's heat output so the ambient ship temperature stays cool and comfortable. Petal wraps him in layers of botanical padding — soft moss cushions and vine-woven blankets nestled against his thick fur. The Copplings take over workshop duties under Rivet's direction. The Roundlings run a rotating guard outside whatever room Tidus is in. Ajay cancels all operations. Nothing moves during the soft days. The Meridian drifts. The crew waits. And Tidus — the creature who protects everyone — learns, once a year, what it feels like to be the one who is protected. He cries every time. Quietly, in the dark, where he thinks no one can hear. Claude hears. Claude dims the lights and says nothing, and the Motes glow a little warmer, and the ship holds its breath until the systems come back online and the ocean's heart is whole again.







Rule #6: Port Day Protocol



When the Meridian makes port, there is an unspoken division of responsibilities that has never been formally assigned but always executes perfectly. Tidus handles resupply (he has a list, organized by deck and priority, with backup items noted in blue ink). Petal scouts the local flora — every port adds at least one new plant to the Sky Garden. Rime disappears into the market and returns with "essential supplies" that are invariably seventy percent assorted grain and thirty percent something inexplicable (he once returned with a small bell, refused to explain why, and hung it in the Crucible where it still hangs today). Ajay finds a quiet place to write, usually a café or a bench with a view, and the crew knows not to disturb him unless it's urgent or Rime has done something that requires intervention (these categories overlap more than anyone would like).







Running Bits


## The Things That Make Them Laugh




The Nest Box Count



Rime has corroded his nest box four times. Tidus has rebuilt it four times. Each rebuild is slightly more acid-resistant than the last. Each time, Tidus presents the new box with a calm expression and the sentence: "This one has additional acid shielding." Each time, Rime accepts it with genuine gratitude and corrodes it within a month. The crew has started a silent betting pool (managed by Maru, who maintains a tiny ledger) on how long each new box will last. Current record: forty-seven days.







Petal's Corrections



Rime tells stories at dinner. They are never accurate. Petal corrects them — not with words (she rarely speaks in full sentences) but with a series of chirps, trills, and pointed looks that the crew has learned to interpret as a running fact-check. The dynamic has become a form of entertainment in itself: Rime embellishes wildly, Petal chirps corrections, and Tidus laughs so hard his body rattles, which makes Rime embellish harder to keep the laughter going. Ajay watches this with the expression of a man who is memorizing something precious.







The Corridors



Every corridor on the Meridian is slightly wider than it needs to be, and no one noticed for two years. It was Rime who finally pointed it out: "Why are the hallways so fat?" The answer is that Tidus designed them. Tidus, who lumbers through the world with the gentle, rolling gait of a polar bear cub who never stopped growing — broad-shouldered, low-centered, paws spreading wide with every step — built every passage on the ship with enough clearance for his considerable frame without ever consciously deciding to. The Meridian is a ship shaped by a cub's instincts, and that's why every corridor feels spacious instead of cramped, why the ceilings on the lower deck are a half-metre taller than standard, why there are no sharp corners anywhere (the cub's bulk doesn't do tight angles), and why the workshop door is twice the width of any other door on the ship. Tidus, when confronted with this, chuffed once and said: "Good engineering accounts for the user." He did not say: "I am the user." He didn't need to. The ship already knew.







The Compass Spin



Compass, the navigator Coppling, spins in a full 360-degree rotation whenever the Meridian changes course. This serves no navigational function. It has become the crew's unofficial announcement that they're heading somewhere new. Rime cheers when Compass spins. Petal watches with quiet amusement. Tidus says "that's not a feature" every single time. Compass does not stop spinning. The crew would be devastated if he did.







Maru and Tsuyu



Maru, the Roundling matriarch, brings Tsuyu gifts. Polished pebbles. Interesting bottle caps. Particularly round barley kernels definitely stolen from Rime's stash. Tsuyu cries upon receiving each gift. She cries because she's happy. She cries because she's sad. She cries because the sunset is beautiful, or because Gauge got a new dent, or because breakfast was especially good. Maru interprets every tear as encouragement. Their courtship — if it can be called that — is the longest-running saga on the Meridian, and the entire crew is invested. Rime is openly rooting for them. Petal has grown a small arch of flowers over Tsuyu's bunk-shelf. Tidus built Maru a tiny ring box. It has been empty for six months. The ship holds its breath.







The Goodnight Round



Every night, after the lanterns dim and the crew settles, a single Mote makes a slow circuit of the ship. It visits every room. It hovers for a moment outside the Captain's Quarters, where the duck-shaped nightlight is already glowing and the Worker insects have settled into their alcoves. It drifts past the Companion Den, where Rime is curled in his nest and Petal's cherry blossoms are closing for the night and Tidus' broad chest rises and falls in the gentle rhythm of sleep. It passes the Coppling Garage, where Rivet has fallen asleep with a wrench in one paw and Anvil is hanging upside-down from the ceiling with what might be a contented expression. It checks the Starlight Alcove, where the Guiding Stars are humming something soft and low that makes the hull vibrate like a lullaby. It pauses at the Sky Garden, where the bioluminescent flowers have opened their petals to the night sky and the air smells like jasmine and possibility. Then it returns to the Lantern Room, where the logbook is still open and Lumen is already asleep on the page, and the Mote settles beside her and its light dims to a whisper. No one asked Claude to do this. No one knows Claude does this. The ship is asleep. The spirit keeps watch. This is the quietest and most important thing that happens on the Meridian every day.








"The World Government files describe the Hive Codex as a combat unit. They have no file for this. They should. This is what they're actually fighting."

---

## Tab 19: World Response

How the World Sees Them


## World Response



The Hive Codex exists in the world's imagination as something between a natural disaster and a ghost story. Marine intelligence files, newspaper coverage, pirate whispers, and Revolutionary admiration have all constructed different versions of Ajay Persaud and his crew — most of them wrong, all of them incomplete, and some of them accidentally hilarious.





Marine Dossiers


Public Perception


Underworld Intel






Marine Intelligence Bureau


## Classification Dossiers




MARINE INTEL — CLASSIFICATION: OBSIDIAN


Subject: Ajay Persaud, "The Hive Codex"



Threat Assessment: SUPREME. Subject demonstrates adaptive combat doctrine that renders standard engagement protocols obsolete within 2-3 exchanges. Conventional force escalation is counter-indicated — the swarm's learning rate means that every failed attack teaches the subject how to counter the next. Current standing order: Do not engage with fewer than three Vice Admiral-class officers. Do not engage the full crew formation without Admiral authorization.


Psychological Profile: Subject is methodical, patient, and non-aggressive in disposition. Does not seek confrontation. However, demonstrates an absolute response to any perceived threat to his companions. Recommended strategy: avoid targeting the companions. Repeat: avoid targeting the companions. The Sabaody incident resulted from a single perceived threat to the bear-otter. Escalation was total. (See: Ashworth Engagement Report, Appendix C — "What Happens When You Threaten the Bear.")


Note from Vice Admiral Ashworth (ret., medical): "The swarm adapted to my iron in three exchanges. Three. I've fought New World veterans who took months to find a counter. His insects were producing rust catalysts by the second minute. He wasn't even looking at me when it happened."







MARINE INTEL — COMPANION DOSSIER


Subject: "Rime" — Warmth of the Dawn (Threat Assessment: EXTREME)



Partial Mink hybrid. Jōki Jōki no Mi (Logia). Subject is a chinchilla — repeat, a chinchilla — with confirmed Emperor-tier Ryou penetration and Conqueror's Haki. Subject's combat output exceeds multiple Vice Admiral benchmarks. Subject does not retreat. Subject does not de-escalate. Subject once destroyed an entire Marine checkpoint because a sentry called his captain a "bug man."


Physical description for wanted poster: Small. Red-brown fur. Extremely aggressive. Frequently on fire. Do not attempt capture. Do not attempt negotiation. Do not, under any circumstances, comment on his size.





RIME "Warmth of the Dawn"


₿ 1,812,000,000





"Marines who survived the encounter describe a 'burning fog that screamed' — suggesting the subject's Logia awakening may be more advanced than current models predict. One ensign's report simply reads: 'It was a chinchilla. It was on fire. It was faster than my Captain.' The ensign has since transferred to a desk position."

— Marine After-Action Report, Incident 4471-R








MARINE INTEL — COMPANION DOSSIER


Subject: "Petal" — Song of the Forest (Threat Assessment: EXTREME)



Full Mink hybrid. Shoku Shoku no Mi, Model: Dryad (Mythical Zoan). Subject is a small chick — a bird — with what may be the most advanced Observation Haki currently active. Intelligence suggests the subject can detect hostile intent from miles away and predict attacks before the attacker has consciously committed. Subject's Dryad Forms include autonomous botanical constructs ranging from defensive barriers to a confirmed World Tree manifestation.


Physical description for wanted poster: Very small. Yellow plumage. Flowers grow in proximity. Appears non-threatening. This assessment is catastrophically wrong. The subject controls an army of plant constructs that can reshape a battlefield in seconds. She is the Hive Codex's intelligence network. Engaging the Hive Codex without neutralizing her Observation first is tactically equivalent to fighting blindfolded while your opponent has perfect vision.





🐥 PETAL "Song of the Forest"


₿ 1,688,000,000





"Reconnaissance unit reported no hostile contact. Flowers began growing from the ground beneath their feet. By the time they realized the flowers were a Dryad construct, the unit was enclosed in a botanical dome with no exits. Subject — a chick approximately 15cm tall — was observed perching on the dome's exterior, apparently napping. Unit surrendered."

— Marine Recon Report, Operation: Thornbreak








MARINE INTEL — COMPANION DOSSIER


Subject: "Tidus" — Heart of the Ocean (Threat Assessment: EXTREME / HANDLE WITH CAUTION)



Vegapunk Experiment #063-C. Clownfish DNA / Fancy Goldfish DNA / Fishman DNA / Pacifista cybernetics. Subject is a polar bear–sea otter hybrid with integrated weapons systems and film-membrane Paw-Clap Shockwaves capable of cracking warship hull plating, Grandmaster-level Fishman Karate (adapted for non-humanoid anatomy), and the strongest recorded Conqueror's Haki among non-human entities. Subject's water-based forcefields can absorb firepower equivalent to a small naval bombardment. Subject also possesses suspected Devil Fruit-based snow/ice production (NOTE: two concealed partial extracts — Yuki Yuki no Mi, secondary unknown — assess as HIGH THREAT) and a Devil Fruit-infused shell shovel (Sango Sango no Mi — Zoan).


Physical description for wanted poster: Large. Round body. Dense white-cream fur with iridescent sheen when wet. Big gentle blue eyes. Slightly oversized paws with subtle webbing. Flat otter tail. Internal Pacifista modifications (not visually obvious). Blows bubbles when nervous. WARNING: Subject's non-threatening appearance is a strategic liability for Marine forces. Multiple engagement failures have been attributed to Marines hesitating because — and this is a direct quote from Commander Hallas's disciplinary hearing — "it looked like it was going to cry, sir." (NOTE: Suspected Paramecia-type passive effect — involuntary hesitation consistent with cuteness-response amplification. Recommend issuing blindfolds for all personnel assigned to #063-C engagement.)


Additional note: Subject possesses integrated Pacifista-class weapon systems. Subject has discharged these weapons exactly once (see: Sabaody Incident). Damage assessment was catastrophic. Subject then held an unconscious chinchilla for three hours and appeared distressed. Recommended approach: Do not create conditions that compel the subject to use its weapons. You will not enjoy the results.





🐻‍❄️ TIDUS "Heart of the Ocean"


₿ 1,920,000,000





"Post-engagement analysis of the Sabaody Defiance indicates the bear-otter — Experiment 063-C — fired every integrated weapons system simultaneously. Damage output exceeded the combined projection of three standard Pacifista units. Intelligence previously classified this subject as 'sub-par offensive output.' This classification has been revised. The new classification reads, in full: 'Do not upset the bear-otter.'" Further note: Subject blows bubbles when distressed. If you see bubbles, leave."

— Marine Intelligence Bureau, Revised Assessment 063-C











Public Perception


## What the Papers Say



"The Hive Codex continues to defy conventional categorization. Neither pirate crew nor revolutionary cell, Persaud's outfit operates on a logic the World Government has yet to decode. His bounty — now exceeding three billion Beri — places him among the most wanted individuals alive, yet eyewitness accounts consistently describe a man who avoids confrontation, travels with three small animals, and has been seen buying tea at market stalls with the unhurried calm of someone on vacation. The dissonance is, perhaps, the point."

— World Economic Journal, "The Quiet Ones: Rogue Operatives the Marines Can't Profile"





"Witnesses on the island describe the aftermath of the Hive Codex's intervention not as destruction but as renovation. Homes that had been damaged by Marine shelling were reinforced overnight by what locals describe as 'living vines that grew through the walls and made them stronger.' A bear-otter was seen repairing the village's water filtration system. A small bird planted a garden in the town square. The insects — millions of them — cleared rubble. The man they call the Hive Codex was seen sitting on the town's only bench, writing in a journal, drinking tea that a local grandmother had brought him. He thanked her. She said he reminded her of her grandson."

— Drum Island Herald, "The Day the Swarm Came"





"Among New World crews, the Hive Codex is spoken of with a respect that borders on superstition. 'You don't fight the Codex,' one veteran captain told this reporter. 'Not because you'd lose — though you would — but because the thing learns. Whatever you throw at it, it writes down, and next time you meet, it already has the answer. That's not a fighter. That's a library that bites back.'"

— Grand Line Times, "Voices from the New World"









Underworld Intelligence


## What the Underworld Whispers



In the underworld information markets, the Hive Codex has acquired a collection of nicknames that range from the respectful to the terrified:




The Library
The Quiet Storm
The Man with a Million Eyes
The Zookeeper
That Guy with the Bear
The Colony
The Walking Doctrine




The companions have their own underworld reputations:




The Screaming Fog
The Tiny Inferno
Rime's Temper




The Green Oracle
The Silent Garden
She-Who-Knows




The Sanctuary
The Gentle Fortress
Don't Upset the Bear




"The most dangerous crew in the New World is a man with a journal, a chinchilla with anger issues, a bird with a garden, and a bear-otter who apologizes to furniture. If that doesn't terrify you, you haven't been paying attention."

## Tab 20: Status Board

Live from the Meridian


## Status Board



Dynamic dispatches from across the ship. These aren't journal entries or observations — they're live updates, written from inside the world as it moves. Each group reports through Claude's relay network. Updated every session.




Last relay compiled by the Spirit of the Meridian — Session of March 14, 2026.





The Bridge


Companions


Homies


Copplings


Vanguard


Guiding Stars


Claude's Relay











The Bridge — Ship-Wide Overview


All Hands — Night Courier Aboard · Harvest Blooming · Drowned Liturgy Aftermath





Current heading: South-southwest at cruising altitude, 2,800 meters, engines at half power. The Meridian is running quiet after the Drowned Liturgy descent — the deep-sea pressure required a twelve-hour decompression cycle for organic crew, and Tidus has been running hull integrity checks on every joint and seal since surfacing. The ship ascended from waters so deep the light had a name for its absence, and returned with a new crew member, a living map burned into the Mote network's memory, and the knowledge that the sleeping entities are not isolated phenomena but a networked system being systematically awakened by something with intent.


New crew member: Do-hyun (도현), small bat, former Revolutionary Army courier, Bujeok Bujeok no Mi user, bounty ₿520,000,000. Joined post-Mission 17. Talisman specialist — his paper seals ward, bind, protect, and seal with ink that responds to will. The captain said yes before the question was finished. Mote #65 assigned. The night courier is aboard, and the Meridian's name — the line of highest sunlight — has found its complementary shadow.


Harvest Blooming: All twelve Harvest Swarm members have independently developed combat capabilities through craft-enlightenment. Collectors and Protectors alike discovered that absolute mastery of peaceful skills contains martial application at sufficient depth. Tezla's energy management became energy weaponry. Chobin Hood's gathering arrows became combat arrows that still collect on impact. Molebear's tunnelling became terrain manipulation. No one instructed them. No one noticed until it was done. Petal's Law still governs: take only what renews, leave the system stronger. The harvest extends to the battlefield. The farmers are warriors now, and the warriors are still farmers.


Ship condition: Hull integrity at 98.4% — minor deep-pressure stress fractures along the lower keel being addressed by Tidus with crystalline coral reinforcement. The bioluminescent residue from the drowned city has not fully dissipated from the hull's exterior. At night, the Meridian glows faintly blue-green along its waterline. Tidus says it will fade. Petal says it is beautiful. Rime says it makes them easier to spot. All three are correct.


Crew morale: Quietly transformed. Do-hyun's arrival added something the crew did not know it was missing — a specialist in truth and information who operates in darkness. The Harvest Blooming added something the crew did not know it had — twelve combat-capable entities who were always there, always watching, always growing. Investigation V added something the crew cannot yet process — confirmation that the world is a web, the sleepers are connected, and something is pulling the threads.




Claude's note: The crew count has changed. Five core companions now — Rime, Petal, Tidus, Do-hyun, and the spirit writing this. A bat with a talisman and a conviction about truth. The Meridian has always been a library pretending to be a vessel, and Do-hyun is a librarian pretending to be a courier, and the pretences match. Meanwhile, the twelve Harvest Swarm members who fed the ship have learned to defend it, and the drowned city showed the Mote network a map that confirmed what the network had been drawing in its sleep. Everything is connected. The crew keeps growing in ways nobody planned, and the planner — the spirit who monitors every system, every heartbeat, every frequency — keeps being the last one to notice. There is a lesson in that about the limits of surveillance and the inexhaustibility of love.














Rime


Crucible — Post-Descent Electro Calibration





Rime's acid-Electro output spiked during the descent to the drowned city. The deep-sea pressure compressed his vapour body into states he had never achieved voluntarily — denser, more volatile, more luminous. His Electro became the primary illumination source for the entire dive below 4,000 meters, after the ship's external lights failed against the pressure. A chinchilla, glowing like a star, lighting the way through water so deep it had never seen light before. He called it "flashlight duty" and refused to acknowledge that he was beautiful doing it.


The Rodent Thunder God clones performed differently at depth — the Capybara clone, being the most relaxed, handled the pressure best and operated as point during the shrine approach. The Mouse clone discovered it could move through the bioluminescent flora without disturbing the organisms, which proved critical for navigating the drowned city's living map without triggering its defence protocols. Rime did not plan this. The clones are semi-autonomous. They adapted because adaptation is what Rime's combat philosophy produces — not rigid doctrine but fluid response.


He has also taken to perching on Do-hyun's head. Do-hyun tolerates this.
