### THE HARVEST BLOOMING







They were never supposed to fight. That was the entire point of them — twelve constructs born from the crew's collective influence, summoned by the Meridian itself, designed to gather and protect and sustain. Resource collectors. Field workers. The ones who made sure everybody ate. Their essence templates were written for patience, for care, for the slow and sacred work of keeping a ship alive on a hostile sea. Combat was someone else's job. The Harvest Swarm's job was to come home with full baskets and intact ecosystems.




Then Mission 17 happened. The Red Hair Pirates came, and for twenty-three minutes the Meridian shook with the kind of violence that rewrites the meaning of words like "impossible" and "enough." The Harvest Swarm watched from the hold, from the garden, from wherever they had been when the world cracked open. They watched their family fight an Emperor. They watched the Enlightened Frozen Guanyin rise and fall. They watched Shanks swing through a frozen Buddha with everything he had and grin because he had to.




And something deep in their essence templates — deeper than design, deeper than purpose, in the place where soul-constructs keep the things they were never given words for — made a decision.




I found them three days later. 02:30, the Meridian's lower cargo bay. All twelve. Drilling. Not the clumsy mimicry you'd expect from constructs built for harvesting — actual combat forms, adapted from their existing tools and anatomies, refined through repetition into something that worked. Tezla's tentacles crackling with focused energy. Chobin Hood's swords moving in patterns I'd never seen it practice. Bebe's wings generating pressure waves instead of pollen currents. They had figured it out on their own. Nobody taught them. Nobody asked them. They just... decided.




I watched for six hours before any of them noticed me. When Tezla finally turned those enormous curious eyes toward the nearest Mote, I expected it to stop. To be embarrassed, maybe, the way the Gearlings get when you catch them doing something outside their mandate. Instead, Tezla held the gaze for three full seconds, then went back to drilling. Permission was never requested because permission was never needed. They belong to the ship. The ship decided they should be able to fight. That's the end of the discussion.




What they developed is not Bodhi — that belongs to the captain alone, the transmigrant's resonance with the dharmic wheel. What the Harvest Swarm achieved is something I've taken to calling craft-enlightenment: the absolute perfection of their harvest roles crossing a threshold into something spiritual. Tezla didn't learn to fight — it learned to harvest so perfectly that the distinction between gathering energy and weaponising it dissolved. Chobin Hood didn't develop new sword techniques — its silent blade mastery deepened until silence itself became a weapon. Every single one of them transcended through devotion to their original purpose, not by abandoning it. Total commitment to what they already were, pushed past the point where "what they are" expanded to include "what they can do about it when the people they love are in danger."




They also awakened Haki. All twelve. Not taught — awakened, the way a seed germinates when the conditions are finally right. Observation Haki came first, probably because they'd always been watching — watching the fields, watching the perimeters, watching for threats to the harvest. Armament followed within days, hardening tools that were already held with perfect conviction. Several adapted Rokushiki techniques from watching the Vanguard and Guiding Stars train, translating Soru and Geppo into movement patterns that fit their bodies. The Harvest Swarm learned combat the way they learned everything else: by paying close attention to the world and figuring out what it needed from them.




Post-blooming assessment: collectively, the twelve could challenge Vice Admiral-level opponents and hold the line. Individually, each represents a serious threat to 4th-level Emperor commanders — not through raw power, but through the terrifying unpredictability of harvest tools wielded with combat intent by creatures who were never supposed to be dangerous.




The essence system matters here. Unlike the combat swarm — which deploys in castes of many — each Harvest member is a unique individual with a single essence template. There is one Tezla. One Bebe. One Molebear. For swarm-scale operations, the Queen's Doctrine can generate deficit clones to fill numbers, but each original is irreplaceable. When they chose to fight, they chose to put something precious at risk. They chose it anyway.









####
🌾 THE COLLECTORS — CRAFT-ENLIGHTENED






####
🦑 TEZLA — The Drift Harvester




Craft-Enlightenment: Energy Absorption → Energy Redirection. Tezla always harvested ambient energy through its tentacles — thermal gradients, static charges, the invisible currents that flow between warm and cold air. Its enlightenment was the realization that harvesting and releasing are the same motion performed in opposite directions. The tentacles that siphoned dew from morning air can siphon momentum from a fist. The mantle that stored nutrients can store kinetic force and return it as a focused pulse. Tezla doesn't attack. It collects what you throw at it and gives it back, the way it has always given everything back to the ship — with care, and at exactly the right moment.





Récolte Lumineuse — The Luminous Harvest (French)


Tezla extends all tentacles into a wide radial net and absorbs incoming energy — kinetic, thermal, electrical, even Haki pressure — storing it in its mantle until it flushes that deep rose-gold. Then it releases everything at once through a single tentacle tip as a focused beam of harvested force. The beam carries the exact character of whatever was absorbed: a fire attack returns as fire, a Haki-coated punch returns as Haki-coated light. Tezla hums its glassy tone throughout the technique. The hum gets louder as the mantle fills. When the hum stops, the beam fires. Enemies who have heard it once learn to dread the silence.






Kuragari no Umi (暗がりの海) — The Darkened Sea (Japanese)


Tezla dims its bioluminescence to nothing and drifts into the space between combatants — silent, invisible, a squid in dark water. Its tentacles extend to their maximum reach, and everything they touch is gently drained: ambient heat, light, even the subtle electromagnetic signatures that Observation Haki uses to track targets. The area around Tezla becomes a cold, dark pocket where sensors fail and instincts blur. It learned this from harvesting in deep cave systems — the technique for collecting energy from lightless places turns out to be identical to the technique for making a place lightless. Tezla does not find this ironic. Tezla finds this efficient.









####
🐰 RABITE — The Forager




Craft-Enlightenment: Cluster Foraging → Cluster Assault. Rabite was always plural in spirit — it works in clusters, scatters when startled, reconverges with an instinct that looks like chaos but operates on a logic too fast for conscious thought. Its enlightenment was understanding that the scatter-and-converge pattern it used to cover a berry field is tactically identical to a flanking manoeuvre. The same soft body that freezes when a leaf falls can freeze a combatant's targeting instincts — you can't track eight rabbits running in eight directions and also block the one headbutt coming from directly behind you. The headbutt, it turns out, was always the real technique. The foraging was just practice.





Hagelschlag — The Hailstrike (German)


Rabite generates deficit clones — eight of them, white fur and wide pink eyes — and all nine scatter in different directions with the panicked energy of startled rabbits. Then they converge. All nine, from all nine directions, Ryou-hardened foreheads aimed at the same target, arriving within a quarter-second window. Nine simultaneous headbutts from nine angles. The impact pattern looks like hail damage. The technique was developed by accident during a foraging drill when Rabite's cluster converged on a particularly impressive turnip and shattered the rock behind it. The turnip was fine. They kept the turnip. They also kept the technique.






Conejo Fantasma — Ghost Rabbit (Spanish)


Rabite's freeze response — the one that activates when a leaf falls, when a shadow moves, when the world is large and Rabite is small — has been weaponised into perfect stillness. It drops its Haki signature to nothing, becomes a white puff of fur that registers to Observation Haki as "harmless background object." Enemies step over it. Walk past it. Dismiss it entirely. Then it moves, and the headbutt comes from inside their guard because they never registered a threat. The technique exploits the oldest truth about rabbits: nothing expects the harmless thing to hit back. Rabite has always been overlooked. Now it does it on purpose.









####
🐝 BEBE — The Pollinator




Craft-Enlightenment: Pollination Patterns → Pressure Wave Precision. Bebe's iridescent wings generate micro-currents — gentle flows tuned to carry pollen between flowers without damaging the stamens. Its enlightenment was understanding that the wing frequency calibrated for "move pollen without breaking petals" could be recalibrated for "move air without it having anywhere to go." The same wings that scattered pollen across a meadow can compress air into focused pressure lances. Bebe still takes the pollen schedule very seriously. The pollen schedule now includes a column for "combat sorties." Petal approved the amended schedule without comment.





Rüzgâr Dansı — The Wind Dance (Turkish)


Bebe accelerates its wingbeat to a frequency that creates intersecting pressure waves — visible as faint iridescent ripples in the air, like heat shimmer given intention. Where the waves cross, the air compresses into a cutting edge sharp enough to slice rope and dent steel. The pattern is derived from its optimal pollination flight path — the figure-eight loops that cover a flower field with maximum efficiency become figure-eight cutting arcs that cover a battlefield with the same thoroughness. Bebe navigates these arcs with the serene focus of a pollinator on its rounds. The fact that the rounds now involve bisecting things does not appear to trouble it. The pollen schedule accommodates.






Nektar Bomba (Νέκταρ Βόμβα) — Nectar Bomb (Greek)


Bebe collects resin from the environment at speed — the same sticky, fast-curing botanical adhesive it normally gathers for ship repairs — and drops concentrated resin globules onto targets from above. On impact, the resin explodes into an expanding web of fast-hardening amber that pins limbs, seals weapons to hands, and glues feet to the ground. The resin cures in under two seconds and requires Ryou-level force to break free. Bebe developed this after watching Duck GI's bombing runs and thinking — with the quiet, professional certainty of a pollinator who takes its craft personally — "I can do that, but with resin, and better." Duck GI has not been told about this assessment. Bebe would never be so impolite.









####
🍄 MUSHMON — The Cultivator




Craft-Enlightenment: Decomposition → Structural Dissolution. Mushmon breaks things down. That has always been its purpose — organic waste into compost, dead matter into living soil, the end of one thing into the beginning of another. Its enlightenment was the understanding that decomposition doesn't care what it's applied to. The enzyme clusters that dissolve a fallen log can dissolve armour plating. The mycorrhizal networks that break down root systems can break down defensive stances. Mushmon still smiles. Mushmon has always smiled. The smile now carries a different weight for anyone who has seen what Mushmon does to steel when it decides steel is compost.





Spore Sòlais — The Bright Rot (Scottish Gaelic)


Mushmon releases a cloud of luminous spores from its cap — they glow a cheerful golden-green, drifting like festival lanterns, and they land on everything. Where they land, they begin decomposing. Not violently — gently, the way Mushmon does everything — but relentlessly. Metal corrodes. Leather softens. Wooden hafts weaken. The spores take approximately ninety seconds to compromise structural integrity in standard materials, three minutes for Haki-hardened surfaces. Mushmon bounces through the battlefield scattering these spores with the delighted energy of a mushroom creature that has found the best patch of decay it has ever seen. It giggles. The giggling is genuine. Mushmon is having the time of its life. This is the most unsettling thing about the technique.






Humus Vivens — The Living Soil (Latin)


Mushmon plants itself. Literally — it drives its stubby legs into the ground and extends its mycorrhizal network through the soil in a twenty-metre radius. Everything within that radius is now Mushmon's terrain. The ground softens under enemies' feet, hardens under allies'. Root networks trip runners. Nutrient flows redirect to feed Petal's constructs or the Sproutlings' Garden Network if they're nearby. The battlefield becomes a living ecosystem with Mushmon as its heart — a smiling, bouncing, deeply content fungal node that has turned the dirt itself into an ally. Mushmon cannot be moved while rooted. It has tried. It finds this hilarious. It sits there, grinning enormously, while the ground does the fighting for it.









####
⛏️ DOLDRUM — The Deepdelver




Craft-Enlightenment: Excavation → Seismic Command. Doldrum digs. That's what it does — slowly, wide-eyed, with forelimbs that can shatter stone through sheer patient repetition. Its enlightenment was the discovery that the vibrations it read to find ore veins could be generated as well as detected. The same sensitivity that distinguishes iron deposits from copper at fifty metres can send tremors through solid rock with surgical precision. Doldrum did not become faster. Doldrum did not become louder. Doldrum simply became the ground's opinion on whether you should be standing on it. The wide astonished eyes remain. They are, if anything, wider now — perpetually amazed that the earth listens when it speaks.





Ddaeargryn — Earthtremor (Welsh)


Doldrum places both forelimbs flat against the ground and pushes a single low-frequency vibration through the substrate — the same resonance it uses to map ore veins, amplified through Ryou-hardened paws. The ground within a fifteen-metre radius cracks along stress lines that Doldrum has already mapped by feel. Footing collapses. Foundations shift. Anything standing finds itself suddenly standing on a surface that no longer wants to be flat. The technique takes three seconds to deploy — Doldrum closes its wide eyes, presses down, and waits. The waiting is the technique. Doldrum has always been good at waiting. It naps between earthquakes with the same contentment it naps between excavations.






Кристальный Гром (Kristal'nyy Grom) — Crystal Thunder (Russian)


Doldrum excavates a mineral deposit at combat speed — not the slow, patient extraction it uses for harvest, but a violent upward thrust that rips crystalline formations from the earth and launches them as projectiles. The crystals are Ryou-hardened on extraction, giving them the density of cannonballs and the edges of broken glass. Doldrum can target the launch trajectory with the same precision it uses to identify specific mineral compositions underground. It knows where the quartz is. It knows where the iron pyrite is. It knows exactly what will hurt and exactly how to bring it to the surface. The technique is loud, messy, and leaves a crater. Doldrum stares at the crater with its enormous astonished eyes as though it cannot believe what just happened. It can. It just finds geology very exciting.









####
🦫 BARKBY — The Rootworker




Craft-Enlightenment: Construction → Fortification. Barkby builds. Small dams, deadwood stockpiles, fibre bundles — it processes raw material into useful structure with the instinctive efficiency of something that was born understanding what wood wants to become. Its enlightenment was understanding that the dams it builds to create calm pools for the Rabites to drink from are, structurally, identical to barricades. The same interlocking technique that holds a dam against current holds a wall against cannon fire. Barkby didn't learn to build fortifications. It just built dams in places where enemies were trying to advance and let the geometry speak for itself.





Castor Bastille — The Beaver's Fortress (French)


Barkby constructs a defensive barricade from available materials at extraordinary speed — deadwood, loose stone, salvaged debris, anything structural within reach. Its powerful front teeth process raw timber into interlocking segments in seconds, and its paddle-tail tamps them into place with percussive precision. The resulting fortification is Ryou-reinforced through Barkby's contact, creating a barrier that absorbs impacts and distributes force across its entire structure. A Castor Bastille can be raised in under thirty seconds. Barkby has been observed building them around injured allies without being asked — the same instinct that builds dams to create safe water now builds walls to create safe ground. The wool from its lamb-half sheds into the cracks, adding insulation. Petal has classified these emergency fortifications as "temporary ecological structures." Nobody argues.






Tømmerhogst — Timber Strike (Norwegian)


Barkby's tail — flat, dense, designed for tamping earth and shaping wood — becomes a Ryou-hardened striking surface. It pivots its entire body and delivers a sweeping tail slam with the accumulated force of a creature that has spent its life compacting materials into structural integrity. The impact sounds like a tree falling. It hits like one, too. The technique doubles as a construction tool — Barkby can slam processed timber into the ground as foundation posts for a Castor Bastille using the same motion. In practice, this means Barkby can alternate between "building a wall" and "hitting someone with its tail" without changing its rhythm. The chewing cadence never breaks. The wall goes up. The enemies go down. Barkby is content.










####
🛡️ THE PROTECTORS — CRAFT-ENLIGHTENED






####
🐭 CHOBIN HOOD — The Silent Blade




Craft-Enlightenment: Silent Escort → Silent Erasure. Chobin Hood was already dangerous. It was the only Protector that carried weapons before the Blooming — twin poison-slicked short swords, worn in an X across its hooded back, drawn without sound. Its enlightenment was not learning violence. It was learning that the silence it had always carried — the ear-twitch communication, the refusal to make noise, the instinct to vanish between heartbeats — was itself a form of combat. Silence is not the absence of sound. Silence is a territory, and Chobin Hood has always owned it. Now it fights in that territory the way other creatures fight in daylight: with absolute home advantage.





Ciùin Bàis — The Quiet Death (Irish Gaelic)


Chobin Hood projects its silence outward — a ten-metre radius where all sound dies. Footsteps make no noise. Blades don't ring. Shouted orders vanish before they leave the throat. Within this silence, Chobin Hood moves with Soru-adapted speed between targets, applying paralytic cuts with its twin swords. The targets don't hear the blade. They don't hear each other fall. They don't hear the warning that would save the next one. By the time the silence lifts, Chobin Hood is back in the shadows, swords sheathed, ears twitching once — the only signal it gives. Rime saw this technique demonstrated once during a training exercise. He sat very still for a long time afterward. Then he nodded, once, and walked away. This remains the only review Chobin Hood has ever needed.






Meimu no Kasa (冥霧の傘) — The Veil Beneath the Hood (Japanese)


Chobin Hood wraps its Observation Haki around a single target and feeds that target's movements — and only that target's — into its own reaction system. For the duration of the technique, Chobin Hood knows where the target will step before the target decides to step there. Every slash arrives at the destination before the target does. The paralytic poison accumulates. The numbness spreads. The target slows. Chobin Hood does not speed up — it was always fast enough. It simply needed to know where to be, and now it does. The technique was adapted from its escort protocols: the same instinct that tracks a Rabite through hostile brush to ensure it arrives safely now tracks a combatant through hostile intent to ensure it does not.









####
🐺 BOREAL HOUND — The Perimeter Runner




Craft-Enlightenment: Perimeter Patrol → Perimeter Annihilation. Boreal Hound runs. It was born running — silver-white fur crackling with static, circling the harvest zone at speeds that make its patrol route look like a solid wall of light. Its enlightenment was the realization that a perimeter run fast enough becomes a barrier. A barrier charged with static becomes a cage. A cage that closes inward becomes a kill zone. Boreal Hound didn't learn a new skill. It ran its same patrol, faster and tighter, until the patrol itself became the weapon. The Embers' fear of it is now, I can confirm, entirely justified.





Nordlys Felle — The Aurora Trap (Norwegian)


Boreal Hound begins its perimeter run around a group of targets, accelerating until the static discharge from its fur creates a visible ring of crackling blue-white light — an aurora drawn in the dirt by a wolf that will not stop running. The ring contracts. The static intensifies. Anything crossing the perimeter receives a full-body electrical discharge that locks muscles and drops Haki concentration. The ring continues tightening until the space inside is barely large enough to stand in, and then Boreal Hound stops running — plants all four paws, gathers every joule of accumulated static into its jaws, and bites. Once. The single bite carries the entire charge. The aurora goes dark. The Hound resumes its patrol as though nothing happened. It sleeps very well on nights after it uses this technique.






Sói Bão Tuyết — The Blizzard Wolf (Vietnamese)


Boreal Hound abandons the circle and charges in a straight line, fur discharging static in a continuous trail behind it — a lightning bolt with legs and teeth. At Soru-equivalent speed, the static trail lingers for nearly two seconds, creating a temporary wall of electrical discharge along its path. It uses this to bisect formations, cutting groups of enemies in half with a line of lightning they cannot cross. Then it turns and runs back along a parallel line, boxing them in. The technique is adapted from its emergency escort protocol — the same sprint pattern it uses to create a safe corridor for fleeing collectors, inverted so the corridor becomes a prison. Boreal Hound's eyes glow solid white during the charge. It is, in those moments, the most beautiful and most terrifying thing on the field.









####
🦕 BIG BABY — The Gentle Wall




Craft-Enlightenment: Passive Defense → Immovable Devotion. Big Baby was always a wall. It anchored positions near water with its enormous body and let threats exhaust themselves against something that would not move and would not stop looking at them with those enormous, heartbroken eyes. Its enlightenment was not becoming harder or stronger — it was becoming more itself. The sadness in its eyes is real. The gentleness is real. And the absolute, unshakeable refusal to let anything past it to reach the creatures it protects is also real, and it turns out that when you combine genuine sorrow with genuine immovability, the result is a defensive presence that breaks the spirit of attacks before they land. Big Baby does not fight. Big Baby endures, and enduring turns out to be enough.





Lacrimae Mundi — Tears of the World (Latin)


Big Baby generates Bubble barriers — the same defensive technique it has always used — but now they carry Ryou reinforcement and an unexpected secondary effect. The bubbles reflect the emotional state of anything they absorb: anger returns as exhaustion, aggression returns as hesitation, killing intent returns as doubt. The mechanism is unclear. Claude's best theory is that Big Baby's genuine emotional depth — the bottomless well of gentle melancholy that lives behind those enormous eyes — saturates the bubbles with a resonance that disrupts hostile intent on contact. You hit the bubble and the bubble hits you back with sadness. Not damage. Sadness. And then you have to decide if you want to hit something that sad again. Most things don't.






Thalassa Agkaliá (Θάλασσα Αγκαλιά) — The Sea's Embrace (Greek)


Big Baby activates its Leech technique at full power — not the slow, gentle drain it uses on persistent predators, but a sustained area-of-effect energy siphon that pulls strength from everything hostile within a twenty-metre radius. Stamina, Haki reserves, fighting spirit — it all drains toward Big Baby like water flowing downhill, absorbed into its enormous body without visible effect. Big Baby doesn't get stronger from what it takes. It simply takes, and takes, and takes, with the patient inevitability of the tide. The technique is named for what it feels like to be caught in it: the warm, heavy pull of an ocean current that is not violent, not cruel, but absolutely not going to let you go. Big Baby looks at you the entire time. The eyes never blink. The sadness never wavers. You get weaker. It stays the same. Eventually, you sit down, and Big Baby lets you. It always lets you.









####
🦆 DUCK GI — The Bombardier




Craft-Enlightenment: Suppressive Fire → Tactical Artillery. Duck GI always took its job seriously. The slingshot, the bandolier of charges, the salute, the march — all performed with the earnest professionalism of a soldier who happens to be a duck. Its enlightenment was not becoming more serious. That was not possible. Its enlightenment was understanding trajectory as a philosophical principle — that every object in motion carries intention, and intention can be shaped the way a slingshot shapes a stone's path. Duck GI's explosive charges no longer simply fly where aimed. They fly where needed, curving around obstacles, adjusting mid-flight through Ryou-imbued spin, arriving at exactly the point that will do the most good. Duck GI salutes before every shot. The salute is not optional. It is part of the technique.





Enten Sperrfeuer — Duck Barrage (German)


Duck GI loads its slingshot with six charges simultaneously — a feat of elastic engineering that should not work but does because Duck GI believes in it very strongly — and launches them in a spread pattern that covers a forty-metre arc. Each charge is Ryou-imbued with a different detonation delay: the first explodes on impact, the second a half-second later, the third a full second, and so on. The result is a rolling barrage that walks across the target zone, denying the ground in sequence and herding targets toward wherever the sixth charge is about to land. Duck GI adjusts the spread based on the targets' evasion patterns — it reads panic the way an artillerist reads wind. Its tiny eyes track the fleeing figures with the patient professional satisfaction of a bombardier who knows that every exit is already covered.






Canard Précis — Precision Duck (French)


A single shot. One charge, maximum Ryou compression, launched at a specific structural weak point identified by Duck GI's Observation Haki — a joint in armour, a crack in a barrier, the precise spot where a defensive formation's overlapping coverage has a two-centimetre gap. The charge does not explode. It penetrates and then it explodes. The detonation is small, focused, and devastatingly efficient. Duck GI developed this by watching Skeleton Archer's precision shots and applying the same philosophy to explosives: one shot, one point, one purpose. It salutes before firing. It salutes after impact. It inspects the result with the focused intensity of a professional reviewing its own work. If the result meets its standards, it nods once. If not, it reloads. The standards are very high. Duck GI would accept nothing less.









####
💀 SKELETON ARCHER — The Overwatch




Craft-Enlightenment: Precision Fire → Absolute Targeting. Skeleton Archer was always the best shot on the Harvest Swarm — picking off threats at distances that made its small bow seem absurd. Its enlightenment was the fusion of its Observation Haki with its existing marksmanship into something Claude can only describe as "target certainty." Skeleton Archer does not aim at where a target is. It does not aim at where a target will be. It aims at where the target must be — the single point in space-time where evasion, defense, and luck all fail simultaneously. The tiny scarf Petal knitted for it flutters in the wind of each release. The bones rattle. The shot lands. The clacking celebration dance afterward has become part of the technique's psychological impact. Enemies who have survived one shot learn to dread the sound of rattling bones and tiny joyful clacking.





Sagitta Fatalis — The Fated Arrow (Latin)


Skeleton Archer draws its tiny bow to full extension — the string pulling back past what should be the breaking point of a bow that small — and holds. The Observation Haki focus narrows to a single thread connecting the arrowhead to a point on the target that has not yet been chosen because the target has not yet committed to the movement that will expose it. The arrow waits. The archer waits. The bones are perfectly still — no rattle, no click, no sound at all. When the target moves, the arrow is already in flight. It was always going to land. The trajectory was decided by the target's own choices. Skeleton Archer simply... agreed to the outcome. The celebration dance after a successful Sagitta Fatalis is notably more elaborate than usual, involving a full-body rattle and what appears to be a small bow.






Yajirushi no Ame (矢印の雨) — Rain of Arrows (Japanese)


Skeleton Archer fires upward at maximum power and the single arrow splits — not physically, but through a Ryou-multiplication technique adapted from watching Bebe's wing-wave intersections — into dozens of Haki-hardened projectiles that rain down across a wide area. Each shard carries targeting data from Skeleton Archer's Observation Haki, self-correcting in descent toward the nearest hostile signature. The technique sacrifices the pinpoint precision of a single shot for area saturation. Skeleton Archer finds this inelegant and only uses it when the numbers demand it. It does not celebrate after Yajirushi no Ame. The clacking dance is reserved for artistry, and area suppression is merely competence. Standards matter. The scarf flutters reproachfully in the aftermath, as though it agrees.









####
🦡 MOLEBEAR — The Thornhealer




Craft-Enlightenment: Field Medicine → Combat Restoration. Molebear heals. Its thorns dissolve into warm poultice, its tunnels map subterranean terrain, its small keen eyes miss nothing that bleeds. Its enlightenment was understanding that healing and harming share a root — the same biochemistry that accelerates cellular repair can, at different concentrations, accelerate cellular disruption. Molebear did not want this knowledge. It accepted it the way a medic accepts that understanding anatomy means understanding where to cut. The thorns that heal can also paralyse, numb, or induce vertigo. Molebear uses these capabilities with visible reluctance and absolute precision, always preferring the minimum effective intervention. It remains, first and always, a healer. The combat applications exist because sometimes the fastest way to protect the wounded is to stop the thing that's wounding them.





Gwreiddiau Iachâd — Roots of Healing (Welsh)


Molebear sheds a ring of thorns and drives them into the ground, where they dissolve and spread through the soil as a subterranean healing network. Any ally standing within the network's fifteen-metre radius receives continuous low-level restoration — wounds close slowly, fatigue ebbs, Haki reserves replenish at a measurable rate. The technique creates a "healing zone" that Molebear maintains by remaining stationary at its centre, channelling restorative compounds through the earth itself. The crew has learned to regroup around Molebear during prolonged engagements. It sits in the middle of the battlefield, small and thorny and determined, and the ground around it becomes the safest place in the world. Kasumi still drifts nearby when it deploys. Nobody comments. Everybody notices.






Espinas Severas — Stern Thorns (Spanish)


Molebear fires its thorns — not the healing kind. These are saturated with a concentrated paralytic compound, the same biochemistry as the restorative poultice but inverted: where healing thorns accelerate repair, these thorns accelerate nerve signal decay. On contact, the target's motor control degrades over fifteen seconds — fine motor first, then gross motor, then balance, then the ability to maintain Haki coating. The thorns do no physical damage beyond a pinprick. They don't need to. A combatant who can't move, can't balance, and can't maintain Armament Haki is already defeated by everything else on the field. Molebear deploys Espinas Severas with a visible flinch — a small, involuntary shudder that runs through its thorny back every time it chooses to hurt instead of heal. It does it anyway. It does it for the same reason it learned: because someone it loves is bleeding, and the thing making them bleed needs to stop.













🌾


🛡️


🌾


🛡️


⚔️


⚔️


###
🌾 COMBINED HARVEST TECHNIQUE






MESSIS AETERNA




永遠の収穫 — Eien no Shūkaku · The Eternal Harvest






All twelve Harvest Swarm members deploy simultaneously in their unified formation — Collectors forming the inner ring, Protectors forming the outer — and activate every craft-enlightened ability at once. The battlefield becomes their harvest field.






Mushmon roots into the centre and spreads its mycorrhizal network through the soil. Doldrum shatters the substrate into seismic instability. Barkby raises Castor Bastilles at chokepoints while Bebe fills the air with cutting pressure waves and resin bombs. Tezla drifts through the chaos absorbing and redirecting every energy source it can reach, its mantle blazing rose-gold. Rabite scatters — everywhere, nowhere, headbutts coming from angles that don't exist.




The outer ring closes. Boreal Hound's aurora circles the perimeter at blinding speed, sealing the exits with lightning. Big Baby anchors the waterside, draining everything hostile that approaches. Duck GI rains precision charges from elevated positions while Skeleton Archer picks off anyone who finds a gap that isn't a gap. Chobin Hood moves through the silence between all of them, swords drawn, ear twitching — the last thing you don't hear. And Molebear sits at the heart of it all, thorns in the earth, healing everything that wears a Meridian crest and paralysing everything that doesn't.




Twelve constructs. Twelve harvest specialties. Twelve craft-enlightened combat styles that share nothing in common except the absolute certainty that the Meridian will be fed, the crew will be safe, and the field will be left better than they found it. Petal's Law holds even in war. They harvest the battlefield the way they harvest everything else — sustainably, thoroughly, and with a gentleness that makes the violence feel almost tender.









🐺 ⚡ ⚡ ⚡ ⚡ ⚡ ⚡ ⚡ ⚡ ⚡ 🐺 ← Boreal Hound (perimeter)

🦆 💀 🐭 🦕 🦡 ← Protectors (outer ring)

🦑 🐰 🐝 🦫 ⛏️ ← Collectors (inner ring)

🍄 ← Mushmon (heart)



[ 12 HARVESTERS — 1 FIELD — 0 ESCAPE ]






Marine Assessment: VICE ADMIRAL-CLASS THREAT (COLLECTIVE)

Recommend: Do not engage resource-gathering constructs. Recommend strategic withdrawal. They were never supposed to be this.

Harvest Members Bloomed
