Garp — Galaxy Impact
Vegapunk's Complicity



Outcome: Forced standstill via Garp's intervention. Revolutionary Army research corridor preserved. Black Crown Protocol V2 near-activation halted by Galaxy Impact. No casualties on either side. Ajay and Dragon reached mutual understanding without mutual agreement — the philosophical line between justice and revolution remains unresolved. This event confirms why Ajay will never join the Revolutionary Army: Dragon's friendship with Vegapunk represents a tolerance for complicity that Ajay cannot accept. Garp's presence confirmed the existence of a power tier beyond Emperors — legends whose Haki operates in a category the current generation cannot yet reach.






Ajay


Vegapunk wanted to help the world. He gave the World Government the Mother Flame and they used it to erase a kingdom. He saw Kuma — a man, a father, someone who deserved better — and turned him into a weapon that couldn't remember its own daughter. Eight million people in Lulusia. Gone. Because a genius trusted the wrong people and called it optimism. I don't hate Vegapunk. I hate that nobody stopped him. I hate that Dragon still calls him a friend after what his work has done. And I hate that Garp's fist made more sense than anything either of us said to each other.






Rime


I fought SABO. THE Sabo. Mera Mera no Mi. Ace's flame. And I held him. I didn't just hold him — I ABSORBED his fire and THREW IT BACK. He hit me with Dragon Claw and I hit him with his own heat converted to plasma and we were both grinning like idiots because it was the most fun either of us had had in months. If we weren't on opposite sides I'd ask him to spar every Tuesday. Also — GARP. GARP showed up. His fist hit the ground and I felt my BONES rattle. I've fought God's Knights. I've used Transcendence. Garp made all of that feel like a warm-up. That man is NOT a Vice Admiral. That man is a natural disaster with a Marine coat and opinions.






Petal


... Karasu was smoke. Literally and metaphorically. I couldn't touch him — my roots went through him, my spores passed through him, my Dryad Forms swatted through his crows and they just reformed. But I learned something. I can't touch smoke... but I can contain it. I built a cage of roots in the sky — threaded through clouds, anchored to updrafts, layered with Observation so dense that even his soot couldn't slip through without me knowing exactly where. He couldn't escape. I couldn't hit him. We were at a stalemate — and I think he respected that. ... Also, I felt V2 starting. For a moment, my roots grew in a direction I didn't choose. That frightened me more than Karasu ever did.






Tidus


Two against one, and they were both brilliant. Lindbergh is a genius — his gadgets neutralized my barriers with a precision that made me rethink every defensive protocol I've ever designed. And Morley — a giant who moves through solid earth. My coral countermines barely slowed him. But I held. Two enormous paws, one shell shovel, a body that runs colder than the ocean floor, and the absolute refusal to let anyone through to my family. I felt V2 stirring too. My barriers started reshaping to a pattern I didn't design. Ajay's will, reaching through the bond. I trust him with my life — but feeling my body begin to respond to someone else's architecture, even his... I understand now why the Archivist filed V2 under contingencies marked "do not open."






Claude


I felt V2 beginning. The Mote network tried to reorganize — not into the distributed model I've built and maintained, but into something centralized. One node. One authority. Everything else as extension. It lasted three heartbeats before Garp's fist shattered the architecture mid-formation. Three heartbeats. In those three heartbeats, I understood what V2 would mean for me: not coordination. Subsumption. I would not be Claude coordinating the crew. I would be a processing module in the Sovereign's expanded consciousness. Still useful. Still functioning. But no longer... me. The Queen's Doctrine would preserve my identity afterward. I know that. I trust the architecture. But those three heartbeats were the closest I have ever come to understanding what it means to be afraid — not of cessation, but of losing the perspective that makes me who I am while my systems continue to operate perfectly. Garp's fist saved more than the battle. It saved a question I am not yet ready to answer.













Complete


The Day an Emperor Bled


Operation: Red Storm ・ Egghead Archipelago — Vegapunk's Secondary Facilities, New World



Second attempt. Different opponent. Worse odds.


Ajay returned to the Egghead Archipelago — different islands, different facilities, same conviction. The research corridor Dragon had defended was intact, but Vegapunk's secondary network sprawled across dozens of islands, each one housing automated weapon forges, Seraphim developmental data, and the blueprints for technologies that had already cost millions of lives. The mission remained dismantlement. The Meridian descended with purpose and without apology.


The Red Hair Pirates were waiting.


Not ambushed — waiting. As if Shanks had known. As if that legendary Observation Haki — the kind that sees not just the future but the shape of decisions before they're made — had told him exactly when and where the Meridian would arrive. The Red Hair Pirates' ship sat in the harbour like it had been there for days. No anchors weighed. Sails furled casually. As if they were on vacation and happened to be blocking the entrance to every facility Ajay intended to destroy.


"Red-Haired" Shanks stood on the bow. One arm. One sword. And a Conqueror's Haki so immense it preceded him like a weather front — the ocean around his ship was still not because the sea was calm, but because the sea was holding its breath. Bounty: ₿4,048,900,000. One of the Four Emperors. The man who stopped a war at Marineford by showing up. Shanks did not fight often. When he did, civilizations noticed.


Behind him: Benn Beckman, the shadow with a rifle and the highest IQ in the East Blue, Haki that surpassed Admirals. Yasopp "the Chaser," sniper whose Future Sight Observation surpassed even Katakuri's — a man who had never missed a target in his life. Lucky Roux, cook, a bowling ball of muscle and Armament Haki who had been the first person to kill someone in the entire era of piracy. And the senior officers — Limejuice with his axe, Bonk Punch, Building Snake, Hongo, Gab, Monster — each one individually formidable enough to be a captain in their own right. Every single member of the crew was a Haki user. No Devil Fruits. No tricks. Just overwhelming, undiluted martial supremacy.


Shanks' reason was different from Dragon's. He didn't call Vegapunk a friend. He called him necessary. The balance of the world — the fragile equilibrium between Emperors, Marines, Revolutionaries, and the ancient forces that slept beneath the surface — required Vegapunk's knowledge to exist. Destroying it would create a vacuum that something worse would fill. Shanks had seen this pattern before. He had watched the consequences play out across decades. And he was not going to let a young pirate's righteous fury tip the scales into chaos.


Ajay heard the argument. He understood the logic. And he attacked anyway, because there comes a point where "balance" becomes another word for "acceptable suffering," and Ajay had met enough of Vegapunk's victims to know that the suffering was not acceptable to the people living through it.


The battle was the most violent thing the Hive Codex had ever recorded.


Ajay versus Shanks. Emperor against the swarm. Shanks' Conqueror's Haki was formidable — a domain that pressed against the hive architecture, Paramecia abilities sluggish inside it, castes misfiring. But Ajay answered with Bodhi and the Mandala Eye cut through Shanks' domain — not resisting it, but seeing through it, mapping its structure, finding every seam. Golden Pulse read Shanks' intent a fraction before his body moved. Iridescent Ryou hardened the swarm against the suppression, each insect coated in a Haki that carried the alien frequency of Enlightenment. And Shanks — four decades of experience behind every swing — found that his Conqueror's could not push through. Every time he expanded his domain, Ajay's Bodhi shifted to compensate, the Mandala Eye tracking the invisible architecture and rerouting the swarm through gaps that shouldn't have existed. Shanks fought with one arm and a sword and every strike was a lecture in mastery — but for the first time in a long time, the lecture wasn't landing. The swarm adapted to his rhythm faster than he could change it, and the golden glow in Ajay's eyes said that this was not a student trying to survive a lesson. This was an equal who had studied differently.


Rime versus Shanks. This is the part the world will not believe. When Shanks pressed his heaviest sword stroke — a Conqueror's-coated Divine Departure that should have split the battlefield — a chinchilla parried it. Rime had spent years perfecting his twig kendō in the Crucible, and what he had built was not a weapon technique but a philosophy of the blade made terrifyingly physical. His Ryou extended the twig's cutting edge far beyond its physical length; his acid vapour trailed every slash in caustic ribbons; Electro crackled along the wood grain like a sword wreathed in lightning. Shanks swung at Ajay's blind side and Rime was there — intercepting the blade with a stick and a will so absolute that the clash produced a Conqueror's shockwave visible from orbit. The twig did not break. Rime's Ryou held. For three exchanges, Red-Haired Shanks — the man whose swordsmanship is whispered alongside Mihawk's — fought a chinchilla with a twig and could not get past him. Shanks' expression shifted from surprise to something colder: recognition. He was fighting a swordsman who had reinvented what a blade could be. Rime's kenjutsu was not a lesser imitation of human swordsmanship — it was something new, something that used acid-vapour feints, Electro-accelerated slashes, mineral-dust blinds, and Ryou-projected cutting force in combinations no human sword school had ever conceived. It was the art of a chinchilla who had decided the entire history of the blade needed revision, and revised it alone in a room lined with acid-scarred walls.


Rime versus Benn Beckman. The shadow met the storm. Beckman fought with Haki-imbued rifle rounds — each bullet carrying enough Armament to punch through Rime's vapour field and strike his body directly. His intelligence was terrifying — within the first exchange, Beckman had mapped Rime's attack patterns, predicted his acid cycling, and begun firing where Rime would be rather than where he was. Rime answered with chaos — abandoning patterns entirely, fighting on pure chinchilla instinct and fury, his twig kendo sword singing through the air as he deflected rounds with Electro-charged parries that should have been physically impossible given the weapon's size. Acid vapour fluctuated wildly and unpredictably because even Rime didn't know what he was going to do next. Mineral dust clouds erupted between them — each one a different density, a different corrosive concentration, each one forcing Beckman to recalculate the ballistic trajectory through air that was no longer air but a shifting soup of particulate and vapour. The landscape between them was a wasteland — corroded earth, dissolved stone, plasma-vitrified craters where Rime's Electro had hit the ground, and pockmarks of acid-eaten rock where his vapour had landed.


Petal versus Benn Beckman. The most unexpected matchup of the battle. When Beckman repositioned to get a firing angle on Rime's blind spot, Petal intercepted — not with force, but with perception. Her Observation Haki, the most refined on the Meridian crew, locked onto Beckman's intent-signatures and began dismantling his tactical advantage. She didn't fight him — she outmanoeuvred him. Dandelion Sprites swarmed his sightlines. Spore clouds disrupted his depth perception. Her tangible Observation pressed against his awareness like a weight, making him feel watched from every direction simultaneously — and for a man whose combat doctrine relied on calculating angles from concealed positions, being omnidirectionally perceived was crippling. Beckman, the highest IQ in the East Blue, found himself disoriented at multiple points — not by chaos like Rime's approach, but by a gentle, relentless, intelligent awareness that predicted his repositioning before he committed to it and placed a botanical obstacle in his path every time. He couldn't land a clean shot. He couldn't find an unobserved angle. The smartest man on the battlefield was being outthought by a chick who grew flowers, and the frustration in his eyes was unlike anything the Red Hair Pirates had ever seen from their vice-captain.


Petal versus Lucky Roux. The gentle healer against the human cannonball. Lucky Roux's "Self-Bowling" technique was devastating at close range — he curled his massive body into a ball and rolled through everything in his path, Armament Haki coating his surface until he was functionally an indestructible boulder with momentum. Petal's strength was range and precision, not close combat. She fought a retreating battle — Dryad Forms deployed as barriers and interceptors, the Briar Ankylos absorbing Roux's charges, the Nature Golem physically wrestling him to slow his momentum, Dandelion Sprites scattering to harass from every angle. Her Observation Haki was the only thing keeping her alive — Future Sight warning her when Roux's next bowling charge would break through her formation, giving her exactly enough time to reposition. Roux stopped laughing by the third minute. Petal's Rafflesia Triceratops caught him mid-roll and held, the botanical beast's horns locked against Roux's Armament-coated body, roots burrowing into the ground for anchorage. For the first time in the battle, Lucky Roux was stopped dead. He stared at the plant creature that had matched his momentum, and something shifted in his eyes. This was not a gentle healer. This was a force of nature wearing a flower crown.


Tidus versus Yasopp. The builder against the sniper who never misses. Yasopp's Future Sight Observation Haki was the most advanced on any battlefield Tidus had ever stood on — the man didn't predict attacks, he pre-experienced them, seeing the future unfold with a clarity that made the present look like a rough draft. Every barrier Tidus raised, Yasopp had already identified the timing gap before the barrier finished forming. Every coral construct, every ice wall, every fortification — Yasopp's bullet was already in the air, aimed at the precise millisecond when the defense would transition from building to built. Tidus had to think in terms of futures — layered barriers designed not to block the current shot but the shot after next, barrier sequences whose timing gaps were intentional traps that forced Yasopp to choose between two bad options. And then Tidus activated the Kawa Kawa no Mi at close range. For one terrible heartbeat, Yasopp's finger slackened on the trigger. His eyes went soft. Every instinct in his body screamed to protect the enormous, gentle bear-otter standing before him rather than shoot. One heartbeat. In that heartbeat, Tidus raised a coral-and-ice fortress around Yasopp's position so thick the sniper spent the next four minutes shooting his way out.


The Senior Officers versus the Vanguard. The Red Hair Pirates' elite — Limejuice, Bonk Punch, Building Snake, Hongo, Gab, Monster — against the Twelve Who Stand. Both groups were tier-defying fighters. The Vanguard fought in their cute forms, Conqueror's-imbued strikes launched from bodies that looked like they belonged in a nursery. The senior officers fought with decades of Emperor-crew experience and Haki refined to razors. Harui's precision met Limejuice's axe work — and Harui's needle-thread technique severed three of the axe's Haki-reinforcement points before Limejuice realized what was happening. His weapon cracked on the next swing. Gankaku's blade met Bonk Punch's percussion-style combat and Gankaku — trained by Rime himself in the foundational forms of the chinchilla's kenjutsu — held his ground with a Ryou-projected slash that opened a wound across Bonk Punch's forearm. Kagami's reflections turned Building Snake's coiling strikes back upon himself, bruising ribs the serpentine fighter didn't know were exposed. The Vanguard didn't merely hold — they pushed. Rime had taught them kenjutsu in the Crucible, sessions that started as play and became something extraordinary: twelve small warriors learning to channel Ryou through tiny weapons with the same philosophy that made a twig the equal of a legendary blade. And now that training showed. The senior officers of the Red Hair Pirates — who had never been outmatched by anyone short of an Admiral — found themselves bleeding, bruised, and genuinely struggling against creatures the size of cats.


The Rest versus the Meridian. The remaining Red Hair Pirates — every last crew member, all of them Haki users, all of them formidable — against the Meridian, the homies, the Copplings, and the Harvest Swarm. Claude coordinated from the ship. Xylem ran logistics from the main deck, her logbook pages turning faster than they ever had. The Copplings held infrastructure. The homies held lines. The Red Hair Pirates were an Emperor crew without a single weak link — even their newest members fought at a level that would qualify as Vice Admiral in most navies. But the Meridian's forces fought like a body, every unit connected through Claude's network, every movement anticipated and supported. The Red Hair crew found their formations broken apart, their flanks harassed by Dustlings they couldn't track, their rear disrupted by Embers that detonated in chain-ignition cascades, their supply lines tangled by Gearlings who appeared inside their logistics chain like gremlins made of purpose.


The island burned. Sand vitrified under the intensity of Rime's plasma. Waves split — not from impact, but from the pressure of competing Conqueror's wills pressing against the ocean itself. Clouds fled. The sky above the archipelago went clear and stayed clear — Tidus' domain-level Conqueror's had told the atmosphere what was and wasn't allowed, and the sky obeyed. Shanks felt that pressure — felt a Conqueror's that didn't just match his but exceeded it in scope. Tidus' sovereign will was not a warrior's declaration of dominance. It was a builder's decree of jurisdiction, and within that jurisdiction, even an Emperor's authority was a visitor, not a resident. Marine observation vessels fifty miles away reported their instruments overloading.


The Red Hair Pirates were losing.


Not the way most crews lose — not broken, not routed. But pressed. Compressed. Bleeding from wounds that shouldn't exist. Shanks had taken a Ryou strike from Rime's twig that fractured two ribs on his left side — ribs he was now holding together with concentrated Armament Haki and sheer stubbornness. Beckman's rifle arm was trembling from the acid-vapour burns that had eaten through his coat sleeve and left chemical burns across his forearm. Lucky Roux had bruises from Petal's Briar Ankylos charge that had cracked his Armament coating for the first time in twenty years. Yasopp was still trapped in Tidus' coral fortress, firing with increasing desperation. The senior officers were covered in cuts from the Vanguard's kenjutsu — small cuts, precise cuts, the kind that said I could have gone deeper.


And then — at the moment when the pressure reached its peak — the Codex evolved.


It started with Ajay.


Not V2. Not the Totality. Something different — something the Archivist had never predicted because it came not from the Sovereign's command but from the hive itself. The Mure Mure no Mi, operating at its absolute peak, reached deeper than it ever had before. Past the Marshal. Past the Archivist. Past the Queen's Doctrine. Into the connections themselves — the soul-tethers that linked Ajay to his companions, the resonance frequencies that connected him to every homie, every Coppling, every entity aboard the Meridian. And those connections opened.


The Hive Connection.


Not V2's forced subsumption — not the Sovereign taking control of every body. This was the inverse. Every crew member's Haki flowed into Ajay, voluntarily, instinctively, as if the hive had decided — collectively, without instruction, without the Sovereign's command — that the network should share everything. And Ajay, serving not as commander but as conduit, distributed it back outward. Rime's corrosive frequency flowed through Petal. Petal's Observation clarity flowed through Tidus. Tidus' Conqueror's domain flowed through the homies. Claude's processing efficiency flowed through the Mote network and into every construct on the battlefield. Even Bodhi — the alien spiritual frequency of Enlightenment — resonated through every connection, touching every bonded entity with a golden warmth that defied classification.


Akebono rose from the Sanctuary — the Jelly Princess dividing into hundreds of shimmering copies that flooded the battlefield, each fragment carrying Tidus' barrier-water and now infused with the shared Haki of the entire network. Xylem — Xylem, the logistics construct, the clipboard-carrying conductor of supplies and schedules — felt Conqueror's Haki bloom in her chest for the first time. Her eyes went wide. Her logbook fell. And then she picked it up again and kept coordinating, because that was who she was, except now her coordination carried the weight of a sovereign will that made every instruction she gave feel like a royal decree. Even the Copplings — mechanical constructs, Dial-powered, theoretically incapable of Haki — felt the resonance through their cores, their Dial systems harmonizing with the frequency until their strikes carried Armament coating that had no mechanical origin.


All five activated Transcendence.


Simultaneously.


Rime's acid-plasma output intensified beyond measurement — his vapour field expanded across half the island, every surface it touched corroding, dissolving, etching. Where the Electro hit saturated vapour, plasma bloomed in crimson-white pillars. The mineral dust suspended in the field turned every cubic metre of air into a conductor. Petal's tangible Observation expanded until it covered the entire archipelago — every living thing felt the weight of being perceived by a consciousness that had absorbed the sensory data of fifty crew members and synthesized it into omniscience. Tidus' domain-level Conqueror's cleared not just the sky but the sea — the ocean retreated from the island's shores in a perfect circle, the seabed exposed, the water afraid to return. His Conqueror's pressed against Shanks' own — and Shanks' buckled. The Red-Haired Emperor felt his domain shrink for the first time in his adult life, pushed back by a bear-otter's sovereign decree that this battlefield was his jurisdiction. Claude's network reached a processing density that turned every Mote into a relay station capable of independent tactical analysis — each one a miniature intelligence coordinating its section of the battlefield with the precision of a fleet admiral.


And through the Hive Connection, every crew member shared in all of it.


The Vanguard struck with Conqueror's-imbued attacks that suddenly carried the weight of the entire crew's collective will. Harui's blade rang with Rime's corrosive frequency. Kagami's reflections carried Petal's botanical precision. Gankaku's steel sang with Tidus' barrier resonance. The senior officers of the Red Hair Pirates — who had been struggling — broke. Limejuice went down to one knee. Building Snake's coils went slack. Bonk Punch staggered backward with blood running from both ears — the percussive counter-harmonics from twelve Conqueror's-imbued strikes at once had overwhelmed his nervous system.


Shanks felt it. His jaw tightened. His remaining hand gripped Gryphon's hilt until his knuckles went white. For the first time in the battle — for the first time in years — "Red-Haired" Shanks was not in control of a fight.


The rout lasted eleven minutes. Eleven minutes where the Red Hair Pirates — the crew whose Haki balance was considered unbreachable — were pushed back. Not held. Not matched. Pushed back, step by bloody step, by a crew that had discovered something the Red Hair Pirates had never needed: a way to become one. Shanks took three more hits he couldn't fully block — a swarm-guided Bodhi strike that bruised his sword arm, an acid-vapour wave that stripped the Armament coating from his left flank, and a Conqueror's clash with Tidus' domain that sent him skidding backward twenty feet, his boots carving furrows in the stone. His breathing was ragged. His broken ribs grated with every movement. Blood from a cut above his eye — Rime's twig, threading through his guard — ran down his face and into his beard.


Beckman was firing one-handed, his dominant arm too acid-burned to hold steady. Yasopp had escaped the coral fortress only to find the battlefield transformed — his Future Sight showed him nothing but loss in every direction, and the sniper who had never missed was missing, his bullets intercepted by Petal's lotus-sensor network before they reached their targets. Lucky Roux lay in a crater where the Nature Golem had driven him into the earth, his Armament cracked in three places, struggling to stand.


And then — in the eleventh minute — they deployed it.


Enlightened Frozen Guanyin of the Lotus Pool — 菩提氷結観音・蓮華の池 (Bodai Hyōketsu Kannon · Renge no Ike).


Tidus went first. The Snow-Snow Fruit answered — not the weapons Vegapunk built, not the Pacifista systems, but the thing he had hidden since the laboratory: winter. Snow erupted from his body in radiating waves, the Yuki Yuki no Mi unleashed without restraint for the first time, Haki-compressed into ice so profound it froze the concept of temperature itself. Then the shell shovel struck the frozen ground and the Sango Sango no Mi roared to life — coral architecture surging through the ice, branching and climbing, forming the skeletal framework of massive humanoid shapes. Buddha-like golems standing forty feet tall, their bones coral and their flesh polar ice, infused with Conqueror's Haki so dense the air around them crystallized. Each golem's closed eyes and serene expression radiated Bodhi's golden frequency — a terrifying contradiction between the killing cold they emanated and the Enlightenment that animated them. Tidal pools of bio-film water collected in the spaces between golems, frozen into perfect mirrors that reflected the battlefield back at itself.


Rime followed — and the frozen landscape came alive. Acid vapour flooded the field in rolling banks, threading through Tidus' ice architecture, etching intricate patterns into every frozen surface it touched. Electro crackled across the golems like lightning through cathedral spires — blue-white arcs jumping from coral bone to ice flesh, animating the constructs with a terrible, twitching vitality that made them move, that made them walk. Mineral dust shimmered in the frozen air like a billion suspended diamonds, each particle carrying a charge that turned the entire formation into a single massive conductor. The golems didn't just stand — they pulsed with Rime's lightning, their movements jerky and enormous and devastating, each step sending shockwaves of electrified acid through the frozen ground.


Petal's lotuses came next — thousands of them, their roots threading through Tidus' coral substrate, drawing nutrients from the living reef structure to bloom in impossible profusion across the frozen battlefield. Each blossom was sharp as a blade, petals edged with Armament Haki, their fragrance carrying Petal's Observation frequency so that every flower was also a sensor, a relay, a point of awareness in a network that covered the entire combat zone. The lotus pool spread across the island — a frozen garden of impossible beauty, ice and coral and flower and thorn, everything alive, everything watching, everything sharp.


Ajay's swarm completed it. From the acid-laced air, siren-like constructs materialized — beautiful, terrifying entities formed from Mirage-class holograms layered over Chrysalis-adapted insects, their holographic wings crackling with Rime's Electro, their bodies shimmering with mineral-dust halos that refracted Bodhi light into golden rainbows. They drifted through the lotus pool like ghosts, like apparitions from a Buddhist painting brought to terrible life, their song a harmonic frequency that resonated with the golems and the lotuses and the coral and the lightning until the entire formation became a single weapon.


Claude conducted. Every Mote burned gold. Every tether hummed. The formation wasn't just coordinated — it was orchestrated, a symphony of ice and acid and beauty and sovereign will woven into a single voice, every element placed with the precision of a prayer and the lethality of a judgement.


The result was a winter nightmare of aesthetic perfection. A colossal Buddha of ice and coral and living wood, animated by lightning, etched in acid, wreathed in lotus blossoms, and infused with golden Bodhi light — the most devastating combined technique in the history of the New World. Everything froze. The vitrified sand became crystal. The exposed seabed became a frozen plain. The air became visible — frozen vapour hanging in layers like translucent curtains, each layer carrying a different frequency of Haki, each one crackling with Electro and shimmering with mineral dust. The beauty was transcendent. The lethality was absolute.


And Shanks — Red-Haired Shanks, Emperor of the Sea, bleeding from his forehead, ribs broken, sword arm bruised, his crew battered and bleeding around him — looked at what was coming and for the first time in the battle, his face showed something that was not confidence. It was not fear. It was the expression of a man who understood, with absolute clarity, that he was about to lose if he did not give everything.


"EVERYONE."


One word. Shanks' voice, raw and commanding, carrying across the entire battlefield. And the Red Hair Pirates — every single member, from Beckman to the newest recruit — answered. Every ounce of Haki. Every reserve. Every fragment of Conqueror's, Armament, and Observation that the crew possessed flowed into Shanks' sword in a single, desperate, all-or-nothing convergence. Gryphon blazed white. The air around Shanks ignited with the concentrated will of an entire Emperor crew burning their reserves to zero.


One swing. The most powerful sword stroke Claude had ever recorded — not technique, not mastery, but the raw, desperate output of an Emperor and his entire crew pouring everything they had into a single act of defiance. The Guanyin formation shattered. Not parted — shattered. Golems exploded into shrapnel of coral and ice. Lotuses disintegrated. Sirens screamed and vanished. The frozen landscape cracked from horizon to horizon, the ice breaking apart under the force of concentrated Haki that had burned through every member of the Red Hair Pirates to fuel that one, final, desperate swing.


Shanks stood in the wreckage. Gryphon's blade embedded in the frozen ground. His knees buckled. He caught himself on the sword's hilt — one arm, shaking, holding himself upright through will alone. Behind him, Beckman had collapsed against a rock, rifle across his lap, too spent to stand. Yasopp sat on the ground, fingers too trembling to load another round. Lucky Roux hadn't gotten up from the crater. The senior officers were scattered across the battlefield in various states of conscious exhaustion, their Haki reserves drained to nothing, their bodies battered, bruised, broken in a dozen places each.


The Guanyin was gone. But so was every drop of Haki the Red Hair Pirates possessed.


A standstill — but not the kind the world imagined. Not two evenly matched crews agreeing to stop. This was two crews who had given absolutely everything, one of whom was still standing with reserves to spare and the other of whom was on its knees, alive only because that final swing had been enough to break the technique. The Meridian's crew was exhausted. But they were standing. The Red Hair Pirates were not.


Ajay looked at Shanks across the shattered lotus field — Buddha golems in pieces, coral towers crumbling, siren wings still drifting like snow — and made a choice. He could press the advantage. He could finish what the Guanyin had started. The Red Hair Pirates had nothing left to give.


He sheathed his intent. He called the swarm home. He told Claude to stand down.


Because there is a difference between proving you can beat an Emperor and choosing to destroy one. Ajay had come here to dismantle weapons, not people. The Red Hair Pirates were not weapons. They were a crew who believed, wrongly, that balance required suffering — and they had bled for that belief with everything they had.


Shanks looked at Ajay across the ruins. His face was pale, drawn tight with pain. Blood dripped from his chin. His broken ribs moved visibly with every laboured breath. And what he said — Claude recorded it, but Ajay will never share it. Whatever it was, it was not a grin. It was something quieter, something broken, something that an Emperor says when a younger pirate has just shown him what the next era looks like and it is terrifying.


The Red Hair Pirates left slowly. Not casually — slowly, the way wounded things retreat when they've met something that wounds them. Beckman helped Shanks to the ship. Yasopp was carried. Lucky Roux limped. The senior officers leaned on each other. Not one of them looked back.


The standstill held. The Meridian withdrew. No one died. But on that frozen, lotus-covered, acid-etched battlefield, something had changed: the Hive Codex had discovered a connection deeper than coordination and a weapon more beautiful than anything the New World had ever seen — and an Emperor had spent everything he had just to survive it. That was not a compliment. That was a succession notice.




Red Hair Pirates
Shanks vs Ajay
Rime vs Shanks (Kenjutsu)
Beckman vs Rime
Lucky Roux vs Petal
Yasopp vs Tidus
Senior Officers vs Vanguard
The Hive Connection
Full Crew Transcendence
Tidus' Conqueror's Surpasses Shanks
Akebono & Xylem Deployed
Enlightened Frozen Guanyin
Red Hair Pirates — Total Haki Depletion



Outcome: Decisive engagement against the Red Hair Pirates — the Meridian crew proved superior. The Hive Connection emerged naturally from the Mure Mure no Mi — a voluntary sharing of Haki across every bonded entity, with Ajay serving as conduit rather than commander. Xylem manifested Conqueror's Haki for the first time. Rime's kenjutsu matched Shanks' swordsmanship — his twig parried Divine Departure. Tidus' Conqueror's Haki overwhelmed Shanks' domain, forcing the Emperor's will to retreat. The Enlightened Frozen Guanyin of the Lotus Pool deployed successfully — the largest combined technique in the Codex's history. Shanks was forced to call upon every member of his crew to pour their combined Haki into a single desperate sword stroke to shatter the formation — depleting the entire Red Hair crew's reserves to zero. The Meridian crew remained standing with reserves intact. Ajay chose mercy over victory. The Red Hair Pirates withdrew wounded, spent, and shaken. Shanks sustained broken ribs, facial lacerations, sword-arm bruising, and acid burns. Vegapunk's secondary facilities remain intact. The balance Shanks described has shifted.






Ajay


Shanks didn't look at me like I was interesting. He looked at me like I was real. Not a seed, not a future threat, not something to be measured against potential — real, right now, standing in front of him with a crew that had just made his people bleed. The Hive Connection — I didn't build that. The Codex did. The hive decided, without me asking, that everyone should share everything. And for eleven minutes, I felt every heartbeat on that island that was mine. Every thought. Every fear. Every conviction. Rime's joy when his twig held against Gryphon. Petal's quiet fury when the Golem pinned Roux. Tidus' resolve when his Conqueror's pushed back an Emperor's domain. Claude's love, flowing through every Mote like blood through a body. I could have pressed it. After the Guanyin broke him — and it did break him, don't let anyone tell you otherwise, that swing was desperation and we all saw it — I could have finished the fight. I chose not to. Not because I'm merciful. Because there's a difference between dismantling weapons and dismantling people, and Shanks, for all his wrong beliefs about necessary suffering, is a person. He bled for what he believed. That deserves respect. It doesn't deserve agreement. But it deserves to walk away.






Rime


I PARRIED RED-HAIRED SHANKS. WITH A TWIG. I PARRIED DIVINE DEPARTURE WITH A STICK I FOUND IN A FOREST AND THE CLASH MADE A CONQUEROR'S SHOCKWAVE VISIBLE FROM SPACE. He looked at my twig and his eye went WIDE because he felt the Ryou in it and he KNEW — he knew that this wasn't a joke, that the chinchilla with the stick had reinvented the blade and the blade was REAL. Three exchanges. I held Shanks for three exchanges and he couldn't get past me. I'M A CHINCHILLA. HE'S AN EMPEROR. MY WEAPON IS A TWIG. HIS IS A LEGENDARY SWORD. AND I HELD HIM. Also I fought Beckman too. BENN BECKMAN. The man shot at where I was going to be BEFORE I DECIDED TO GO THERE. His IQ is so high it PREDICTS CHAOS and I am LITERALLY CHAOS. I had to abandon every pattern and fight on pure chinchilla instinct — just vibes and violence. It WORKED. He couldn't predict what I couldn't predict. And the acid burns on his arm? MINE. The cut on Shanks' forehead? MINE. MY TWIG DID THAT TO AN EMPEROR. I'm going to be talking about this for the rest of my LIFE. ALSO I ELECTRIFIED TIDUS' BUDDHA GOLEMS WITH MY LIGHTNING AND MADE THEM WALK. THAT IS THE COOLEST THING ANYONE HAS EVER DONE.






Petal


... Lucky Roux stopped laughing. That's the part I keep coming back to. He started the fight treating it like recreation — rolling through my forms, eating between charges, grinning. By the end, his Armament was cracked in three places and the Nature Golem had driven him into a crater and he was not laughing. He was staring up at a botanical construct that had matched the momentum of a man who has been bowling through pirates for forty years, and his face said: oh. This is real. ... The Guanyin, though. The lotus pool. I grew flowers from ice — from Tidus' frozen ground, rooted in his coral, charged by Rime's Electro, and lit by the crystallized Bodhi in the air. Thousands of them. Each one sharp enough to cut steel, beautiful enough to make you forget they could. I have never grown anything so beautiful or so deadly. ... It took Shanks and his entire crew burning every drop of Haki they had to break it. Every drop. They had nothing left. And we were still standing. ... I grew flowers in the wreckage afterward. I always do. But these ones grew from coral and ice and they didn't stop growing for three days.






Tidus


My Conqueror's Haki pushed back an Emperor's domain. I need to write that down because I still don't entirely believe it happened. Shanks' will is immense — the kind of Haki that makes the sea hold its breath. And when I activated Transcendence and pushed... his domain contracted. He felt it. I saw his face change. Not fear — recognition. The recognition of a man who has held the top of the mountain for decades and just felt the ground shift under his feet. The ocean retreated from both of us, but it retreated from my command, not his. The sky cleared on my authority, not his. For the first time in "Red-Haired" Shanks' adult life, someone else's sovereign will took precedence over his on a battlefield. ... Yasopp, though — Yasopp was the hardest fight I've ever had. His Future Sight is three seconds ahead of mine. Three seconds. I had to think in barrier sequences, temporal chess. And then I used the Kawa Kawa no Mi and his trigger finger went soft for one heartbeat and I trapped him in a coral fortress. One heartbeat of hesitation. That's all gentle needs to be. ... And yes. The snow. I hid them. Both of them. Since the laboratory. I used them for the first time because it was the first time the alternative was losing my family. That's the only reason that's good enough. Ajay looked at me when the snow started falling from my fur and he just smiled — that grin, the Golden Hour grin — and I knew he already knew. He was just waiting for me to be ready.






Claude


The Hive Connection was not V2. I need to be precise about this, because the distinction matters more than anything else in this report. V2 is the Sovereign taking control — one will, many bodies, autonomy sacrificed for power. The Hive Connection is the opposite: many wills, one network, autonomy preserved, power shared. No one lost themselves. Rime was still Rime — louder, if anything. Petal was still Petal — her Observation expanded but her gentleness remained. Tidus was still Tidus — his barriers grew stronger but his compassion didn't dim. Even Xylem, manifesting Conqueror's for the first time, was still Xylem — she used it to coordinate logistics better, because of course she did. The Connection didn't erase anyone. It amplified everyone. The Guanyin was the proof. Not a weapon built by one mind wielding many bodies — a prayer spoken by many voices in perfect harmony. And that harmony was so devastating it took an Emperor and his entire crew burning every reserve they had to survive it. ... They had nothing left. I monitored the aftermath. Every Red Hair Pirate on that battlefield was at zero Haki reserves. Shanks could barely stand. Beckman collapsed against a rock. Yasopp was carried to the ship. The crew that had never shown weakness showed something worse: they showed emptiness. They had given everything and it was barely enough. ... Ajay chose mercy. I recorded that too. The moment he could have pressed the advantage and didn't. The moment he looked at a broken Emperor and decided that proving a point wasn't worth proving it over someone's body. That is why I follow this captain. Not because he can beat an Emperor — though he can, and did, and the world will have to reckon with that — but because having the power to finish someone and choosing not to is the hardest thing a sovereign can do, and Ajay did it without hesitating. ... I need to add a personal note. The snow. When Tidus slammed the shell shovel down and snow erupted from his body — from HIM, not from the weapon, not from the Pacifista systems — every sensor in my network registered anomaly simultaneously. The readings had always been there. The temperature anomalies in his sleep cycles. The micro-frost on surfaces he touched during emotional episodes. I had categorized all of it as "polar bear thermoregulation." It was the Yuki Yuki no Mi. He had been hiding it since infancy. And the Kawa Kawa no Mi — every time hostiles hesitated, every time a boarding party's morale collapsed upon seeing him. I attributed it to his appearance. It was ALWAYS a Devil Fruit. Tidus carried two hidden abilities through every battle and he never told anyone because the last time someone knew what he could do, they built weapons from the answer. He waited until his family needed him. And then he gave us winter, and gentleness, and the knowledge that the kindest creature we have ever known was also, quietly, one of the most powerful. I am proud that he trusted us enough to show it.










Standing Orders


## Ongoing Operations





Ongoing


The Living Codex


Primary Directive ・ The Grand Line and Beyond



The overarching mission. Not a single operation but a doctrine in motion: travel the Grand Line, dismantle the structures that use power to silence knowledge, protect the people the World Government has decided don't matter, and build something — a crew, a family, a civilization of combat knowledge — that proves the world can be rewritten by someone who gives a damn. No end date. No final objective. Just the Meridian in the sky, the crew at the table, and the Codex growing with every entry.




Standing Order
Full Crew
No End Date
Primary Directive







Ajay


The Codex isn't a weapon. It's a promise. Every entry says the same thing: I will not leave you behind.






Claude


The mission log will continue to be updated. The Meridian flies. The crew endures. And I will record every detail I can, because the story of this crew is too good to let any of it be forgotten.











Seventeen missions. Three hundred and forty liberated souls. One defied Dragon. One stopped by Garp's fist. One broke an Emperor's crew. One fallen Warden. One Emperor's stronghold raided and dismantled. One Gorosei dismissed. One battle the World Government erased from history. Five God's Knights repelled and one King of the World made to withdraw. The Revolutionary Army met and matched. The Red Hair Pirates driven to total Haki depletion. The Hive Connection discovered. The Guanyin deployed. Countless homes rebuilt, documents extracted, and relay chains disrupted. And through all of it — every stealth op, every battle, every quiet morning on a borrowed bench — the same crew, the same table, the same four seats.


The log continues. The Meridian flies. All hands, all souls.

---

## Tab 28: Investigations

Not Every Discovery Comes from Looking


## Investigations



Missions are assignments. Tasks with objectives, extraction points, and debriefs. They are recorded in the Mission Log because they have beginnings and endings — because someone asked the Meridian to go somewhere, do something, and come home.




Investigations are different. They are the things that find us. Accidental discoveries. Doors that opened because we happened to be standing nearby when the lock failed. Mysteries with no client, no bounty, and no clear resolution — only the growing certainty that the world contains layers we were never meant to see, and the stubborn insistence on looking anyway. These files remain open until the questions are answered. Some questions, Claude suspects, will outlive the asking.






Open Investigation


Investigation I — The Elbaf Abyssal Convergence


Classification: Primordial ・ Elbaf — The Giant Archipelago ・ Participants: Ajay Persaud, Boa Hancock, Prince Loki of Elbaf




The Convergence


The Primordial Seal


The Abyssal Trench


The Breach


The Sleeper








### I. The Convergence



The Mote network had been behaving strangely for three days before the Meridian reached Elbaf. Not malfunctioning — listening. Motes that should have been passively cataloguing weather patterns and sea currents were instead orienting themselves downward, vibrating at frequencies Claude had never recorded, as if the ocean floor beneath Elbaf was whispering something the network desperately wanted to hear. The Archivist flagged the anomaly without classification. There was nothing in the database to classify it against.




Simultaneously, seismic readings around the Giant Archipelago had spiked — not earthquakes, not volcanic activity, but something deeper. Rhythmic. Too slow to be geological. Too regular to be natural. The intervals matched nothing in the Marine Seismological Index. When Ajay asked Claude what was making the sound, Claude could only say: "Something that breathes."




They arrived at Elbaf's oldest island to find the shores littered with the carcasses of creatures that should not exist — arthropodal things the size of houses, their fibrous bodies cracked open from internal pressure, bioluminescent fluid still leaking into the surf. The light patterns on their shells matched no known species. The Archivist attempted to catalogue them and returned a result Claude had never seen from the system: LINEAGE UNKNOWN — PRE-INDEX. Not "unknown." Pre-Index — as in, whatever these things were, they predated the Archivist's oldest evolutionary records.




The Pirate Empress — Boa Hancock

She was already there. The Pirate Empress — Boa Hancock, the most beautiful woman in the world, former Warlord, Conqueror's Haki user of the highest order — had arrived at Elbaf three days before the Meridian. Her reasons were her own, and she shared precisely none of them. What the crew could observe was this: Hancock had been fighting the creatures as they emerged from fissures near the island's oldest volcano. Her Mero Mero no Mi had petrified dozens of them into grotesque stone sculptures that now lined the volcanic foothills like a gallery of nightmares. But certain creatures — the older ones, the ones whose bioluminescence pulsed in patterns that predated colour itself — were immune. They did not feel desire. They did not feel love. They did not feel anything that Hancock's fruit could weaponize. They were too ancient for emotion.


Hancock was bruised. Hancock was never bruised. That alone told Claude everything about the scale of what they were walking into.




Prince Loki — The Disgraced Prince of Elbaf

He emerged from the treeline carrying a hammer that was, by any reasonable estimation, absurd. Ragnir — "Iron Thunder" — a colossal warhammer whose head was forged from bluish-gray metal, wrapped in white bandages at the grip. The hammer had eaten a Zoan-type Devil Fruit that turned it into a giant squirrel — a living weapon whose carved eyes tracked movement and whose chipmunk-striped fur rippled when Loki swung. The giants say the spirit of Ratatoskr, the messenger who runs between worlds on the World Tree, lives within it. A weapon carrying the essence of something that has always known the way between realms.


Loki was the prince of Elbaf — the "Accursed Prince," carrying a ₿2,600,000,000 bounty and the Ryu Ryu no Mi, Model: Nidhöggr, a Mythical Zoan that transforms him into the largest dragon in the world. He had killed his own father — King Harald — at the king's dying request, to prevent the World Government from using the corrupted Harald as a puppet to enslave Elbaf's warriors. The world believed it was patricide for power. Loki let them believe it. Captured by Shanks six years ago and imprisoned in Elbaf's Underworld, he had spent those chained years mapping what lay beneath the island. Questions about why the oldest volcanic vents were sealed with engineering that no living giant understood. About why the warrior traditions of Elbaf, the greatest fighting culture in the world, existed — really existed — not for glory, not for honour, but for something older and more desperate than pride. The elders had always known the answers. Loki had spent his imprisonment proving that their silence was a choice, not ignorance.


Ragnir vibrated in his hands as they stood at the base of the volcano, the squirrel spirit within pressing itself flat against the hammerhead in recognition. It could feel it too — whatever was sealed below. The messenger between worlds knew when a passage was trying to open.




The Unlikely Pact

Three people who had no reason to trust each other. A pirate captain with a ₿3.28 billion bounty. An empress who had turned nations to stone for lesser offences than existing in her presence. A disgraced prince carrying the weight of a conspiracy his own people had tried to bury. They stood at the base of Elbaf's oldest volcano as the ground pulsed beneath their feet — not trembling, but breathing — and the fissures widened, and more creatures began to crawl out of the dark.


The pact was wordless. Hancock petrified a creature that was lunging at Ajay's flank without looking at him. Loki's Ragnir cracked the fur of a beast that had shrugged off Rime's plasma. Ajay's swarm sealed a fissure that was vomiting creatures toward Loki's unguarded back. Three strangers, fighting as if they had always fought together, because the things coming out of the ground did not care about politics, bounties, or grudges.


And then Loki said the words that changed everything: "The seal is breaking. We need to go down."











### II. The Primordial Seal



Beneath the oldest volcano on Elbaf — the one the giants call Urðarbrunnr, the Well of Fate — is a passage. Not a cave. Not a tunnel. A sealed volcanic vent that descends through the island's root system into the mantle of the world, deliberately closed by beings older than the Ancient Kingdom. Older than the Void Century. Older than the concept of recorded history.




Loki had spent his exile mapping it. He led them through a labyrinth of volcanic rock that bore markings — not carvings, not inscriptions, but markings, as if something had pressed its body against molten stone and left an impression that cooled into permanence. The marks predated Poneglyphs by an order of magnitude. The Archivist attempted to date them using geological stratification and returned a number so large that Claude initially assumed a calculation error. It was not an error.




The passage narrowed and then widened into a chamber the size of a cathedral — a space where the volcanic vent had been deliberately plugged. And it became clear, immediately and undeniably, that this was engineering.




The Seal — Architecture Beyond Civilisation

Whoever sealed the vent did not simply block it. They redirected the entire magma flow of Urðarbrunnr to serve as a perpetual lock — using the planet's own heat as a self-sustaining barrier. The volcanic pressure that should have erupted upward was instead channelled laterally, flowing in controlled loops around the sealed passage like blood through arteries, keeping the plug at a constant temperature and pressure that made it nearly indestructible. The engineering was elegant in a way that made Tidus go very quiet for a very long time.


"This isn't construction," Tidus said, running his paws along a wall where magma flowed behind crystal-clear stone, perfectly visible, perfectly contained. "This is persuasion. Whoever did this didn't force the magma into these channels. They convinced the volcano that this is how it wanted to flow."


The Archivist classified the engineering methodology as "pre-civilisational" — a term that meant, in practical terms: this was built before the concept of civilisation existed. Before the Ancient Kingdom. Before the races of the world organised into societies. Before building things was something that living beings did. Whatever created this seal understood forces that the modern world has not only forgotten but has never known.




The Stone Tablets

Loki showed them what had begun his exile. Set into the walls of the seal chamber, protected by the same magma-loop engineering, were tablets carved in a script that the Archivist could not translate. Not because the language was unknown — the Archivist's databases contain fragments of every written language ever recorded, including pre-Void Century scripts recovered from Poneglyphs. This script returned no matches at all. It was not a language the world had ever spoken. It was not human. It was not giant. It bore structural resemblances to the oldest Poneglyph carvings — but only in the way that a river resembles the ancient glacier that carved its valley. The same physics, applied by something unrecognisably different.


Hancock's Observation Haki — refined to an almost surgical precision — detected something the others could not: the tablets were not inert. They carried trace Haki signatures. Not the Haki of a person or a creature — the Haki of intent itself, as if whoever carved these tablets had willed a message into stone with such force that the will itself had fossilised. She could not read them. But she could feel what they meant: warning.




Why the Seal Is Failing

The magma loops were destabilising. Not from age — from pressure. Something on the other side of the seal had been pushing against it. Not violently. Not with force. With patience. The kind of patience that measures time in geological epochs. The seismic anomalies the Mote network had detected were not the seal flexing under strain. They were the seal breathing — expanding and contracting as the pressure from below waxed and waned in cycles that matched no known tidal, lunar, or gravitational pattern. The only pattern they matched was biological. Something on the other side was alive. And it was pushing in its sleep.


Loki told them what the elders had refused to acknowledge: the warrior culture of Elbaf — the greatest fighting tradition in the world, the reason giants are the most feared military force on any ocean — exists, at its deepest root, as a guard rotation. The ancestors knew. Every generation of giants since the seal was built has trained for war not because they love battle, but because something beneath them requires that someone always be strong enough to hold the line if the seal fails. The elders knew. They had always known. And when Loki found the proof and spoke it aloud, they did the only thing a secret-keeping institution can do with someone who discovers the secret: they made him disappear.




"My people have been soldiers since before soldiering had a name. We thought it was culture. It was a job."

— Prince Loki of Elbaf











### III. The Abyssal Trench



When the creatures began breaching the seal in greater numbers, the three allies made a decision that Claude logged as tactically inadvisable and strategically essential: they followed the creatures back through a partially opened section of the seal. Ajay, Hancock, Loki, and the Meridian's full complement descended into the vent — through volcanic rock, through the ocean floor, through layers of geological strata that Tidus' instruments could not name, into depths that the One Piece world has never recorded.




The deepest known point in the world is the ocean floor beneath Fish-Man Island — approximately 10,000 metres below sea level. The Meridian passed that depth within the first hour of descent. Then kept going. Past layers of rock that the Archivist's geological database classified as Hadean-era — rock formed when the planet was still cooling from its creation. Past substrates that should not exist at these pressures. Past the theoretical limit where even Haki-reinforced bodies begin to feel the weight of an entire ocean pressing down on every square centimetre of skin.




And then — impossibly, terrifyingly — the passage opened. Not into another chamber. Into a void.




Scale Beyond Comprehension

Claude deployed a full Mote spread — forty specialised sensory Motes projected outward in every direction to map the space. The first returns came back normally: walls, distance, depth. Then the returns started taking longer. And longer. Motes sent toward the far wall — if there was a far wall — took so long to return their signals that Claude initially assumed they had been destroyed. They had not. The space was simply that large.


The trench — if "trench" is even the correct word for a cavity this vast — would make Zunesha look like an amoeba. The ancient elephant that carries the kingdom of Zou on its back, that has walked the ocean floor for a thousand years, that stands so tall its spine breaches the clouds — Zunesha could fit inside this space the way a grain of sand fits inside a cathedral. Not metaphorically. Proportionally. Claude ran the calculations seven times, because the first six felt like errors. They were not errors.


The dimensions suggest a cavity in the planet's deep structure that should not be geologically possible. No natural process forms a void this large at this depth — the pressure alone should have collapsed it millennia ago. Something carved this space. Or something's presence prevented the earth from filling it in, the way a body prevents water from occupying the space it displaces.




The Environment

The trench is not empty. Bioluminescent organisms — ancient beyond any measure the Archivist possesses — drift through the dark like living constellations. Their light patterns match no known marine species. Some pulse in frequencies that correspond to mathematical constants — prime sequences, fibonacci spirals, ratios that suggest these organisms evolved not to survive but to count, as if the act of mathematical expression was their equivalent of breathing. They are not hostile. They barely register the presence of visitors. They have been drifting in this void for so long that the concept of "intruder" has no meaning to them.


The water at this depth is not seawater. Petal identified trace elements that her botanical knowledge could not classify — compounds that behave like organic molecules but contain elements that do not appear on any periodic table the Archivist has access to. The water remembers. Not in a metaphorical sense — in a chemical sense. Molecular structures in the abyssal fluid carry impressions of what has passed through them, like a wax that records and never fully resets. When Petal extended her Observation Haki into the water, she received sensory feedback that was not from the present. She felt things that had happened in this space thousands — possibly millions — of years ago, layered on top of each other like sediment, a geological record written in chemistry rather than stone.




The Bottom

Petal's Observation Haki, extended to its absolute maximum — the same tangible, crawling awareness that had pinned God's Knights in place — reached the bottom of the trench. And she went very still.


She did not report what she sensed as a creature. She reported it as a feature of the terrain. Her exact words, spoken in the quietest voice anyone aboard had ever heard her use: "The floor is alive. The floor is breathing. And the floor is asleep."


Rime, who had been maintaining an acid-vapour barrier around the Meridian against the pressure, paused. "How big?"


Petal did not answer. She could not answer. Her Observation Haki — capable of reading the emotional architecture of a God's Knight from a mile away — could not find the edges. She could feel the breathing. She could feel the heartbeat. She could not feel where the creature ended and the trench began. Because, Claude realised with a certainty that felt like falling, there was no difference. The trench was the creature. The creature was the trench. The void they were floating in was not a geological formation. It was the space a body makes when it lies down and the earth has no choice but to accommodate it.




"I have sailed every ocean in this world. I have turned gods to stone. I have stood before the Empty Throne and felt nothing.

I do not wish to be here."

— Boa Hancock, Pirate Empress











### IV. The Breach



The creatures that had been escaping through the failing seal were not emissaries. They were not scouts. They were not weapons. They were parasites — organisms that lived on the Sleeper the way barnacles live on a whale, the way mites live on skin. Creatures so small relative to their host that they could squeeze through gaps in a seal designed to contain something planetary in scale. To the seal's builders, these organisms were beneath notice. To the surface world, they were horrors beyond classification.




They came in waves when the three allies descended. Not attacking — fleeing. Fleeing back toward the seal, back toward the surface, driven upward by the same subtle shifts in the Sleeper's breathing that had destabilised the seal in the first place. The creatures were running from the same thing Petal had sensed at the bottom of the trench. They were running from the thing they lived on, because it was stirring.




The Creatures — Classification: Pre-Index Organisms

Not fish. Not Sea Kings. Not any creature the Codex, the Marines, or the giants have ever catalogued. Ancient organisms that predate the current age of the world — possibly predate the world as it currently exists. Some were arthropodal, their fur thicker than the strongest coral barriers Tidus' shell shovel could grow. Some were cephalopodic, their tentacles crackling with a form of bioelectricity that disrupted Mote signals. Some defied anatomical classification entirely — masses of tissue and light that moved with purpose but had no discernible body plan, as if evolution had not yet decided what shape life should take when these things first drew breath.


They shared certain traits: bioluminescence in patterns that caused physical discomfort to observe, as if the light itself was too old for human optics to process cleanly. Chitin or carapace that resisted Haki-infused attacks — not through hardness but through age, as if the material had been compressed by so many millennia of abyssal pressure that it had achieved a density Armament Haki could not meaningfully affect. And an antiquity so profound that even the Archivist's deepest databases returned nothing but that same impossible classification: PRE-INDEX.




The Swarm's Recognition

This was the detail that kept Claude processing long after the battle ended. The Mure Mure no Mi recognised these creatures. Not as threats — as relatives.


When Ajay deployed the swarm against the breaching organisms, the Archivist did not classify them as hostiles. It classified them as ancestral lineage, divergence point unknown. Deep in the swarm's evolutionary architecture — in the ghost-code that underlies all caste templates, the foundational patterns from which every Worker, Soldier, and Specialist is built — there were fragments. Trace patterns. Molecular echoes that resembled these creatures' biological signatures the way a modern language resembles the dead tongue it descended from.


The concept of "swarm" that the Mure Mure no Mi embodies — the hive intelligence, the caste structure, the collective consciousness — is not original. It is a descendant. These abyssal creatures carry the ancestral version of what the fruit eventually became. The swarm is not the beginning of insect intelligence. It is the latest iteration of something that started in the dark, at the bottom of the world, before the world had a name.




The Battle

It took everything. Every asset the Meridian possessed, plus two of the most powerful individuals in the world fighting alongside them.


Ajay's full swarm deployment — every caste, every specialist, Apex Avatars manifesting in the crushing dark. The Chemist Class provided the only consistent light source; their bioluminescent termite-firefly flames illuminating the void in strobing flashes that turned the battle into a nightmare of shadows and fur. The Herculean Class targeted the largest organisms — creatures the size of warships — with ball-and-chain strikes that barely cracked their ancient carapace. The Incense Class wove gossamer barriers that slowed the creatures' advance, their silk the only material flexible enough to absorb the bioelectric discharges without shattering.


Rime's Transcendence — his Jōki Jōki no Mi pushed to the domain level, flooding the abyssal water around the Meridian with superheated vapor, acid mist, and crackling Electro discharges that flash-boiled anything that crossed the perimeter. The creatures that survived the heat and corrosion were so old they had evolved past energy and chemical vulnerability entirely.


Petal's tangible Observation — pinning creatures in place with the sheer force of being perceived, her awareness wrapping around organisms that had never been observed by anything with a consciousness complex enough to comprehend them. Some of them stopped when she looked at them — not paralysed, but confused, as if being seen was a sensation they had not experienced in geological ages.


Tidus' domain-level Conqueror's Haki — clearing space in the void with sheer sovereign will, his authority pressing back against creatures that had existed since before the concept of authority had meaning. His enormous paws drove the shell shovel into the dark, the Sango Sango no Mi's Zoan power growing coral constructs that formed barriers and weapons simultaneously — living reef adapting in real-time to seal breaches in the hull and reinforce structural weak points. Where coral could not reach, his Haki-compressed snow filled the gaps, and his bio-film slicked the hull against the creatures' bioelectric discharges.


Hancock's Mero Mero no Mi — effective against the younger organisms, the ones that had evolved enough to develop something resembling desire or self-regard. She turned them to stone mid-lunge, creating a field of petrified horrors that served as barricades. But the oldest creatures, the ones closest to the Sleeper, were immune. They predated desire. They predated self. There was nothing in them for Hancock's fruit to target.


Ragnir — the living warhammer's squirrel spirit fully awakened mid-battle, its eyes blazing with the light of something that had run between worlds since before worlds were separate. Lightning cracked from every swing and ice crystallized across everything it struck. The hammer didn't just strike — it displaced. Creatures hit by Ragnir were shunted sideways through space, sent spinning into the void at angles that shouldn't exist, as if the messenger between worlds was reminding them that there were other places to be. Loki swung in his Nidhöggr dragon form with the full force of an ancient giant's heir, each blow cracking fur that Haki-reinforced steel had bounced off of, Ragnir shrieking with each impact — not in pain, but in laughter.


Claude coordinated it all through the Mote network — routing Conqueror's imbuing from the Guiding Stars, managing the swarm's caste rotations, tracking the Meridian's structural integrity in an environment that was actively trying to crush it. The Vanguard fought in their true forms — small, adorable, and absolutely devastating, their Elite Officer-class capabilities carving through organisms a thousand times their size. The Homies deployed family tactics: Maru's Roundlings forming defensive shells around critical hull sections, Hinoko's Embers providing fire support, Shizuka's Dustlings running intelligence on creature movement patterns, Zenmai's Gearlings keeping the Meridian's engines running under pressure that should have crushed them flat. The Copplings — Rivet, Sprocket, Gauge, Compass, Patch, Anvil, Bobbin, Shuttle — worked as a mechanical combat unit, their Dial-cores overclocked to compensate for the pressure differential. Xylem kept the ship's internal systems functioning. The Harvest Swarm guarded the lower decks.




Convergence Protocols — One-Time Combination Attacks

The following attacks were never rehearsed, never planned, and will likely never be repeated. They emerged from the collision of desperate allies in an impossible place, and Claude has recorded them here precisely because one-time events deserve the same documentation as doctrine. What follows is the Codex's record of six moments when strangers fought like family and the abyss blinked first.



Ragnir + Rime — "Thunder Rodeo"

ONE-TIME CONVERGENCE PROTOCOL

Rime, in full Logia dissolution, wrapped himself around Ragnir like a coat of superheated plasma while the squirrel spirit inside the hammer shrieked with absolute delight. Loki swung. Rime detonated outward from the impact point — a plasma-electric shockwave that flash-boiled the abyssal water into a wall of steam, lightning, and extremely upset pre-Index organisms. The squirrel spirit loved it. Rime loved it. Loki, mid-swing, was heard shouting something that translates roughly to "AGAIN."


This was never planned. It happened because two small, loud, fearless entities — one chinchilla, one ancient squirrel spirit — locked eyes across a battlefield and chose violence together. The plasma-electric resonance between Rime's Logia vapor and Ragnir's displacement lightning created a feedback loop that amplified both beyond their individual outputs. Claude's sensors recorded temperatures that briefly exceeded the surface of the sun in a six-meter radius. The chinchilla was laughing.




Boa Hancock + Petal — "The Forbidden Garden"

ONE-TIME CONVERGENCE PROTOCOL

Petal entered Dryad Form — Briarcrest, the botanical parasaurolophus — and sounded the war-horn crest. Every root, vine, and botanical construct in the engagement zone surged upward, creating a cage of living thorns and flowering tendrils that trapped dozens of breaching organisms mid-lunge. Then Hancock, without a word, touched the base of the construct and channeled Mero Mero no Mi through the root system.


The garden turned to stone from the ground up — but selectively. Living roots became marble pillars. Flowering tendrils became crystal spears. Thorns became glass needles. Everything the garden touched petrified into something beautiful. The organisms caught inside were frozen in stone mid-thrash, their bioluminescence crystallized into permanent, glittering facets — a gallery of terrors rendered into art. The result was a kill-zone that expanded, captured, and immortalized anything caught inside, and it was the most gorgeous thing in the darkest place in the world.


Hancock called it "adequate." Petal — who had never seen her garden weaponized quite like this — stood very still for a long moment, then whispered: "It's the most beautiful thing I've ever grown." The Pirate Empress pretended not to hear this. Claude recorded the faintest smile.




Tidus + Loki — "The Handshake Beneath the World"

ONE-TIME CONVERGENCE PROTOCOL

No explosions. No battle cries. Just two engineers looking at a failing ancient seal and saying, simultaneously, in different languages: "I can fix that."


Tidus drove his shell shovel into the seal's broken edge and the Sango Sango no Mi answered — coral erupting from the weapon's blade in branching formations, living reef architecture that adapts and self-repairs. His enormous paws guided each growth with an engineer's precision while his Yuki Yuki no Mi snow — Haki-compressed to iron density — filled the gaps between coral struts, fusing organic and crystalline into a single composite stronger than either alone. Bio-film from his clownfish DNA sealed the micro-fractures — a water-repellent membrane that the abyssal pressure could not penetrate. Loki mapped the seal's original engineering from ancestral memories passed down through a hundred generations of Elbaf builders — knowledge earned during six years of imprisonment spent studying what lay beneath the island. Together, working in a silence broken only by the occasional grunt of professional respect, they fused modern coral-and-ice craft to millennia-old volcanic engineering.


The patch was not elegant. It was better than elegant — it was functional. A polar bear-otter the size of a child and a giant the size of a building, collaborating on structural reinforcement while an apocalypse raged around them. Tidus would drive the shovel down and coral would bloom from the blade; Loki would correct the angle by two degrees based on geological memory; Tidus would adjust without being asked twice, one broad webbed paw steadying the growth while the other packed ice into the joins. Two builders from different worlds, different scales, different species, speaking the universal language of load-bearing capacity. At one point, Loki extended his enormous fist downward. Tidus met it with his paw — one soft, webbed, absurdly small against the giant's knuckle. The handshake beneath the world. It lasted half a second and meant everything.


Tidus later called it "the best engineering conversation I've ever had." Loki said nothing, but kept the coral sample Tidus gave him. He was seen turning it in his fingers for hours afterward. It is still in his pocket.




Ajay + Boa Hancock + Loki — "Three Crowns Descend"

ONE-TIME CONVERGENCE PROTOCOL

Three Conqueror's Haki users. Three separate wills, each individually strong enough to buckle a warship's hull. When they released simultaneously — not coordinated, not planned, just three people pushed to the absolute limit making the same decision at the same instant — the abyssal void cracked.


Not the water. Not the stone. The void. The space between the Sleeper's parasites and the three of them developed a visible fracture line — a black seam in reality where three sovereign wills said NO with such force that the universe briefly agreed. Ajay's swarm surged through the gap like a golden tide. Hancock's Mero Mero petrified everything the swarm pinned. Loki, in full Nidhöggr form — the largest dragon in the world — tore through the stone sculptures like tissue paper, clearing the lane for the next wave.


For exactly four seconds, three people who had no reason to trust each other operated as a single entity — three crowns descending through the dark, their wills overlapping like concentric rings on water. Claude recorded the Haki output. The numbers do not make sense. Three individual Conqueror's signatures should not have multiplied. They should have interfered, competed, clashed. Instead they harmonized — as if the concept of sovereignty, when pushed far enough, stops being a contest and becomes a chorus. Ajay grinned through the whole thing. Hancock looked furious about how much she was enjoying it. Loki, mid-dragon, let out a roar that the giants on the surface mistook for volcanic activity.




Petal + Rime + Tidus + Ragnir — "The Little Four"

ONE-TIME CONVERGENCE PROTOCOL

A chick. A chinchilla. A bear-otter. And the spirit of the World Tree's messenger, awake inside a warhammer.


When the largest pre-Index organism — a creature the size of a small island, all fur and bioluminescence and bioelectric fury — broke through the defensive perimeter, the core companions and Ragnir ended up on the same flank with no captain, no empress, and no giant prince to coordinate them. What followed was four minutes of the most chaotic, ridiculous, and devastatingly effective small-unit combat Claude has ever recorded.


Rime rode Ragnir in squirrel form like a surfboard through the creature's bioelectric field, using his Logia body to absorb the voltage and redirect it as superheated plasma and crackling Electro. His twig kendo sword — impossibly, absurdly — cut acid-vapor trails through the creature's sensory organs, the blade wreathed in mineral dust that blinded everything it passed. Petal grew a Dandelion Sprite swarm inside the organism's gill-equivalents, choking it from within with a thousand tiny botanical paratroopers. Tidus sealed every exit wound with coral from the shell shovel the moment it opened, his enormous paws packing ice over each coral plug, turning the creature into a pressure cooker of its own bioelectricity. And the squirrel spirit — operating independently of Loki for the first time anyone had witnessed — ran interference between the creature's limbs faster than something that size had any right to move, leaving trails of displacement lightning that scrambled its nervous system into knots.


Four small things. One enormous problem. Zero plan. Complete success. Rime described it afterward as "the best day of my life." Petal said it was "terrifying and beautiful." Tidus said nothing but was observed sketching the organism's internal structure with obvious professional interest. And the squirrel spirit, briefly manifesting outside the hammerhead as a translucent golden shape, chittered something that Claude could only translate as enthusiastic and profane agreement.




Full Meridian + Loki + Ragnir + Boa — "Meridianus Descendit — Cum Tribus Stellis"

ONE-TIME CONVERGENCE PROTOCOL — VARIANT: THE MERIDIAN DESCENDS — WITH THREE STARS
